questions.explanation: One of the three main generators must have been on line before both AC main buses became unpowered.

questions.question: The EICAS warning message indicating loss of all normal electrical power and ADG deployment is:
questions.explanation: EMER POWER ONLY indicates the ADG is supplying the AC essential bus and no power is available from AC BUS 1 or 2.

questions.question: The PWR TXFR OVERRIDE switch is used when:

options.A: You want to manually deploy the ADG
options.B: Normal AC power has been restored and you want to reset the emergency transfer contactors
options.C: You want to tie DC BUS 1 and 2 together
options.D: You want to test the ADG lamp only

questions.explanation: PWR TXFR OVERRIDE de-energizes the emergency transfer contactors if main AC power has been restored.

questions.question: For the transfer contactors to reset after pressing PWR TXFR OVERRIDE, the ADG manual deploy handle must be:
questions.explanation: The manual deploy handle must be stowed to allow the transfer contactors to reset.

questions.question: Selecting the ADG test switch to UNIT checks:

options.B: Continuity of the uplock release circuit, transfer contactors, and auto-deploy logic
options.C: Only the ADG propeller pitch mechanism

questions.explanation: UNIT tests the uplock circuit, the transfer contactors, and the ADG auto-deploy control unit logic.

questions.question: Pulling the ADG manual deploy handle will:

options.B: Release the uplock and immediately energize the emergency AC/DC and hydraulic pump 3B transfer contactors
options.C: Connect the ADG directly to AC BUS 1

questions.explanation: Manual deployment releases the uplock and energizes the Emergency AC Transfer, Emergency DC Transfer, and Hydraulic Pump 3B Transfer contactors.

questions.question: If the ADG fails and the airplane is on battery power only, all electrical power may be lost after approximately:
questions.explanation: The PTG notes that all electrical power may be lost after 30 minutes on battery power only.

questions.question: The DC system is normally powered by:
questions.explanation: The normal sources of 28-volt DC power are the four TRUs.

questions.question: Each TRU is rated at:
questions.explanation: Each TRU is rated at 100 amps.

questions.question: TRU 1 normally supplies:

options.A: DC BUS 1 and DC UTIL BUS 1
options.B: DC BUS 2 and DC UTIL BUS 2
options.C: DC ESS BUS and BATT BUS
options.D: DC EMER BUS and MAIN BATT DIR BUS

questions.explanation: TRU 1 normally supplies DC BUS 1 and DC UTIL BUS 1.

questions.question: TRU 2 normally supplies:

options.A: DC BUS 1 and DC UTIL BUS 1
options.B: DC BUS 2 and DC UTIL BUS 2
options.C: DC ESS BUS and BATT BUS

questions.explanation: TRU 2 normally supplies DC BUS 2 and DC UTIL BUS 2.

questions.question: ESS TRU 1 and ESS TRU 2 normally supply:

options.B: DC ESS BUS and BATT BUS

questions.explanation: ESS TRU 1 and ESS TRU 2 normally power the DC ESS BUS and the BATT BUS.

questions.question: If TRU 1 or TRU 2 fails, DC BUS 1 and DC BUS 2 can be tied together by selecting:
questions.explanation: Selecting MAIN BUS TIE ties DC BUS 1 and DC BUS 2 together so either remaining TRU can power both buses.

questions.question: Whenever MAIN BUS TIE is selected following a TRU 1 or TRU 2 failure, the DC utility buses are:

options.D: Tied to the AC utility buses

questions.explanation: The DC utility buses are load shed to reduce the DC electrical load.

questions.question: The main battery is a:
questions.explanation: The main battery is a 24-volt, 17-amp/hour NiCad battery.

questions.question: The APU battery is a:
questions.explanation: The APU battery is a 24-volt, 43-amp/hour NiCad battery.

questions.question: The battery chargers are in a charging configuration whenever:

options.A: Either battery voltage is below 18 volts
options.B: The applicable main AC buses are powered
options.D: The BATT MASTER switch is OFF

questions.explanation: The main and APU battery chargers charge whenever the applicable AC buses are powered.

questions.question: The main battery charger is powered from:
questions.explanation: The main battery charger uses AC BUS 1.

questions.question: The APU battery charger is powered from:
questions.explanation: The APU battery charger uses AC BUS 2.

questions.question: MAIN BATT DIR BUS and APU BATT DIR BUS are continuously powered:

options.A: Only when BATT MASTER is ON
options.B: Only when AC power is available
options.C: Regardless of BATT MASTER switch position

questions.explanation: Both battery direct buses are continuously powered from their respective batteries regardless of BATT MASTER position.

questions.question: The EICAS warning message EMER POWER ONLY means:

options.A: Only external AC power is available
options.B: The ADG is supplying the AC ESS bus and no power is available from AC BUS 1 or 2
options.C: Only the battery bus is powered
options.D: The APU generator is over 34.5 kVA

questions.explanation: EMER POWER ONLY means the ADG is supplying the AC essential bus and no power is available from AC BUS 1 or 2.

questions.question: The Challenger 604 hydraulic system consists of:
questions.explanation: The aircraft is equipped with three independent hydraulic systems designated 1, 2, and 3.

questions.question: All three hydraulic systems operate at a nominal pressure of:
questions.explanation: All hydraulic systems operate at a nominal pressure of 3000 psi.

questions.question: The hydraulic systems power all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: The hydraulic systems power flight controls, landing gear, wheel brakes, and nosewheel steering, but not engine starting.

questions.question: Each hydraulic system has:

options.C: A main pump and a backup pump

questions.explanation: Each system has two pumps: a main pump (A) and a backup pump (B).

questions.question: Systems 1 and 2 main pumps are:
questions.explanation: System 1 and 2 main pumps are engine-driven pumps (EDPs), designated 1A and 2A.

questions.question: System 3 main pump is:
questions.explanation: System 3 main pump is an AC motor pump (ACMP) designated 3A.

questions.question: All backup pumps 1B, 2B, and 3B are:
questions.explanation: All backup pumps are AC motor pumps (ACMPs) designated 1B, 2B, and 3B.

questions.question: The major components of Systems 1 and 2 include an engine-driven pump, an AC motor pump, a hydraulic shutoff valve, an accumulator, and a:
questions.explanation: The major components of Systems 1 and 2 are an EDP, an ACMP, a hydraulic shutoff valve, an accumulator, and a reservoir.

questions.question: All major components of Systems 1 and 2 are located in the aft equipment bay except the:
questions.explanation: All components are located in the aft equipment bay except the EDP, which is located on the engine.

questions.question: The main pumps in Systems 1 and 2 draw fluid from the reservoir through the:
questions.explanation: The main pump draws fluid from the reservoir through the firewall shutoff valve.

questions.question: EDP 1A and 2A operate anytime:

options.D: The HYD panel switch is ON

questions.explanation: EDP 1A and 2A operate anytime their respective engine is running.

questions.question: Backup pumps for Systems 1 and 2 are designated:
questions.explanation: The backup pumps for Systems 1 and 2 are ACMP 1B and 2B.

questions.question: Hydraulic pumps 1B and 2B are controlled by:
questions.explanation: Pumps 1B and 2B are controlled by three-position toggle switches labeled AUTO, ON, and OFF.

questions.question: With pump 1B or 2B selected to AUTO, the pump automatically starts when its respective bus is powered, the associated cross-side engine-driven generator is operating, and:

options.A: The aircraft is on the ground
options.B: The flaps are greater than 4 degrees

questions.explanation: AUTO operation requires the bus powered, cross-side generator operating, and flap position not at zero (greater than 4°).

questions.question: The normal switch position for hydraulic pumps 1B and 2B in flight is:
questions.explanation: AUTO is the normal switch position for flight operations.

questions.question: ACMP 1B and 2B do not automatically start after:

options.C: An engine failure or an EDP failure

questions.explanation: The PTG specifically notes that ACMP 1B and 2B do not automatically start after an engine failure or an EDP failure.

questions.question: With pump 1B or 2B selected ON, the pump will operate provided:

options.A: The respective bus is powered and the associated cross-side engine-driven generator is operating

questions.explanation: In the ON position, ACMP 1B and 2B operate provided their respective bus is powered and the associated cross-side engine-driven generator is operating.

questions.question: On the ground, an interlock circuit allows pumps 1B and 2B in ON to operate even if:

options.B: The engine-driven generators are not operating

questions.explanation: On the ground, the pumps can operate with the switch ON even if the engine-driven generators are not operating.

questions.question: During normal operations, ACMP 1B operation is controlled by the output of:
questions.explanation: Pump 1B operation is controlled by GEN 2 output.

questions.question: During normal operations, ACMP 2B operation is controlled by the output of:
questions.explanation: Pump 2B operation is controlled by GEN 1 output.

questions.question: In the event of an in-flight engine-driven generator malfunction or engine failure, the control logic inhibits operation of the opposite-side ACMP. This is called:
questions.explanation: This function is called load shedding.

questions.question: The purpose of hydraulic load shedding is to:

options.B: Prevent an electrical overload during single-generator operation

questions.explanation: Load shedding prevents an electrical overload condition during single-generator operation.

questions.question: Electrically operated hydraulic shutoff valves are installed in the suction lines of:
questions.explanation: Hydraulic shutoff valves are installed in the suction lines of EDP 1A and 2A.

questions.question: Hydraulic shutoff valve positions are indicated on the:
questions.explanation: Valve positions are indicated on the EICAS Hydraulic synoptic page.

questions.question: The hydraulic shutoff valves for Systems 1 and 2 are normally:
questions.explanation: The hydraulic shutoff valves are normally open.

questions.question: During an engine fire, the corresponding hydraulic shutoff valve is motored closed by pressing:

options.A: The hydraulic pump switch to OFF

questions.explanation: The corresponding shutoff valve is motored closed by pressing the ENG FIRE PUSH switch/light.

questions.question: Each hydraulic system accumulator is precharged with nitrogen to:
questions.explanation: Each accumulator is precharged with nitrogen to 1500 psi.

questions.question: The purpose of each hydraulic accumulator is to store nominal pressure and:
questions.explanation: Each accumulator satisfies instantaneous demand and dampens pressure surges.

questions.question: System 1 reservoir capacity is approximately:
questions.explanation: System 1 reservoir capacity is 2.95 liters (0.78 U.S. gal.).

questions.question: System 2 reservoir capacity is approximately:
questions.explanation: System 2 reservoir capacity is 3.93 liters (1.04 U.S. gal.).

questions.question: Systems 1 and 2 reservoirs are filled via the ground service panel located:

options.B: Directly below the hydraulic equipment rack in the aft equipment bay
options.C: In the right main wheel well

questions.explanation: Each reservoir is filled via the ground service panel directly below the hydraulic equipment rack in the aft equipment bay.

questions.question: During preflight, hydraulic inspection should include checks for fluid leaks and accumulator pressure at:
questions.explanation: During preflight, accumulator pressure should be checked at 1500 psi.

questions.question: Hydraulic systems are serviced with:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the systems are serviced with Skydrol-B.

questions.question: Skydrol-B is easily identified by its:
questions.explanation: Skydrol-B is identified by its purplish color.

questions.question: Skydrol-B is cautioned as being:

options.B: highly corrosive and irritating to skin and eyes

questions.explanation: The PTG cautions that Skydrol-B is highly corrosive and can produce severe skin and eye irritation.

questions.question: The major components of System 3 are:

options.A: One EDP, one ACMP, a reservoir, and a shutoff valve
options.B: Two AC motor pumps, an accumulator, and a reservoir
options.D: One ACMP, one RAT pump, and a reservoir

questions.explanation: System 3 consists of ACMP 3A and 3B, an accumulator, and a reservoir.

questions.question: ACMP 3A and 3B are located in the:

options.B: left and right side fillets of the aft belly fairing

questions.explanation: ACMP 3A and 3B are located in the left and right side fillets of the aft belly fairing.

questions.question: The System 3 accumulator, reservoir, and ground service panel are located in the:
questions.explanation: The System 3 accumulator, reservoir, and ground service panel are located in the right main wheel well.

questions.question: The main pump for System 3 is:
questions.explanation: The main pump for System 3 is AC motor pump 3A.

questions.question: ACMP 3A is controlled by a:
questions.explanation: Pump 3A is controlled by a two-position ON/OFF switch.

questions.question: Pump 3A operates continuously when selected ON, provided:
questions.explanation: Pump 3A operates continuously with the switch ON, provided AC BUS 2 is powered.

questions.question: The backup pump for System 3 is:
questions.explanation: The backup pump for System 3 is ACMP 3B.

questions.question: With 3B selected to AUTO, it operates when AC BUS 1 is powered, either engine-driven generator is operating, and:

options.B: The flaps are greater than 4 degrees

questions.explanation: AUTO logic for 3B requires AC BUS 1 powered, either engine generator on-line, and flaps greater than 4°.

questions.question: ACMP 3B does not automatically start after:
questions.explanation: The PTG notes that ACMP 3B does not automatically start after ACMP 3A failure.

questions.question: With ACMP 3B selected ON, it will operate provided:
questions.explanation: With 3B selected ON, it operates provided AC BUS 1 is powered.

questions.question: During ADG deployment, ACMP 3B is automatically energized:

options.D: Only after PWR TXFR OVERRIDE is selected

questions.explanation: During ADG deployment, ACMP 3B is automatically energized regardless of switch position.

questions.question: During ADG deployment, ACMP 3B is powered from the:

options.B: ADG BUS through the Hydraulic Pump Transfer Contactor

questions.explanation: ACMP 3B is energized by the ADG BUS through the Hydraulic Pump Transfer Contactor.

questions.question: If AC power is restored after ADG deployment, ACMP 3B returns to normal operation when:

options.D: The ADG handle is pulled again

questions.explanation: If AC power is restored by an engine-driven generator or APU generator, ACMP 3B returns to normal operation when PWR TXFR OVERRIDE is selected.

questions.question: If ADG deployment is caused by a double engine failure, ACMP 3B provides emergency hydraulic power primarily to the:
questions.explanation: In a double engine failure, ACMP 3B provides emergency hydraulic power to the primary flight controls.

questions.question: To ensure priority to the primary flight controls, a spring-loaded closed priority valve cuts off System 3 pressure to the landing gear selector valves below:
questions.explanation: The priority valve cuts off pressure to the landing gear selector valves if System 3 pressure drops below 2100 psi.

questions.question: If the System 3 priority valve cuts off landing gear selector valve pressure, the crew will require:
questions.explanation: Below 2100 psi, alternate landing gear extension will be required.

questions.question: System 3 reservoir capacity is approximately:
questions.explanation: System 3 is the largest, with a reservoir capacity of 11.14 liters (2.94 U.S. gal.).

questions.question: During preflight, with the exception of the accumulator gauge, System 3 components are not readily accessible when:

options.B: The wheel well bins are installed

questions.explanation: System 3 components are not readily accessible with the wheel well bins installed.

questions.question: Hydraulic pump 1B in AUTO operates in flight whenever flaps are extended and:
questions.explanation: Pump 1B AUTO logic uses cross-side GEN 2.

questions.question: Hydraulic pump 2B in AUTO operates in flight whenever flaps are extended and:
questions.explanation: Pump 2B AUTO logic uses cross-side GEN 1.

questions.question: Hydraulic pump 3B in AUTO operates whenever flaps are extended and:
questions.explanation: Pump 3B AUTO logic requires either engine generator on-line.

questions.question: The EICAS caution HYD EDP 1A means:

options.B: Engine-driven pump 1A pressure is less than 1800 psi
options.C: Hydraulic shutoff valve 1 did not close

questions.explanation: HYD EDP 1A indicates engine pump 1A pressure is less than 1800 psi.

questions.question: The EICAS caution HYD 3 LO PRESS means:

options.B: System 3 pressure is less than 1800 psi

questions.explanation: HYD 3 LO PRESS indicates system pressure is less than 1800 psi.

questions.question: The EICAS advisory HYD SOV 1 CLSD means:

options.A: Hydraulic shutoff valve 1 failed to close
options.B: Hydraulic shutoff valve 1 has closed after actuation of the respective ENG FIRE PUSH switch/light

questions.explanation: HYD SOV 1 CLSD indicates the shutoff valve has closed after ENG FIRE PUSH actuation.

questions.question: The EICAS caution HYD 1 HI TEMP means:

options.B: System 1 temperature is greater than 96°C, or greater than 107°C for 4 minutes after takeoff power is applied

questions.explanation: HYD 1 HI TEMP indicates system temperature is greater than 96°C or greater than 107°C for 4 minutes after takeoff power is applied.

questions.question: The Challenger 604 fuel system consists of:

options.A: A tank system, a transfer system, and a dump system only
options.B: A fuel tank system, a fuel distribution system, a fuel transfer system, and a refueling/defueling system
options.C: Only a fuel tank system and engine feed system
options.D: A tank system and an electrical management system

questions.explanation: The PTG states the fuel system consists of a fuel tank system, fuel distribution system, fuel transfer system, and refueling/defueling system.

questions.question: The Fuel System Computer Unit (FSCU) monitors fuel quantity and controls:
questions.explanation: The FSCU monitors fuel quantity and controls fuel transfer and refueling/defueling.

questions.question: During normal flight operations, fuel management is:

options.D: Controlled only by the crew through crossflow

questions.explanation: The PTG states that during normal flight operations, fuel management is automatic and requires no crew input.

questions.question: The fuel system storage consists of:

options.A: Main tanks, collector tanks, and vent tanks
options.B: Main tanks, auxiliary tanks, and tail tanks
options.C: Wing tanks and center tank only
options.D: Left, right, and fuselage tanks only

questions.explanation: The PTG lists three tank systems: main tanks, auxiliary tanks, and tail tanks.

questions.question: The total fuel capacity achievable by pressure refueling is approximately:
questions.explanation: The PTG gives total fuel capacity as 20,000 lb / 9098 kg for pressure refueling.

questions.question: The total fuel capacity achievable by gravity refueling is approximately:
questions.explanation: The PTG gives total fuel capacity as 15,722 lb / 7130 kg for gravity refueling.

questions.question: The left and right main tanks are:
questions.explanation: The PTG describes the left and right main tanks as integral-type fuel tanks (wet wings).

questions.question: Flapper valves in the wing ribs are used to:

options.B: Prevent fuel from surging toward the wing tips

questions.explanation: Flapper valves in the wing ribs prevent fuel from surging toward the wing tips.

questions.question: The APU’s normal fuel supply is from the:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the APU’s normal fuel supply is from the right main tank.

questions.question: The collector tanks are considered part of the:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the two collector tanks are considered part of the main tank system.

questions.question: The auxiliary tank system consists of:

options.A: Left and right wing tip tanks
options.D: The tail cone and two saddle tanks

questions.explanation: The auxiliary tank system consists of forward, center, and aft auxiliary tanks.

questions.question: The auxiliary tanks are located:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the auxiliary tanks are located in the fuselage below the cabin floor.

questions.question: The auxiliary tanks are:
questions.explanation: The three interconnected auxiliary tanks are filled and drained simultaneously.

questions.question: The auxiliary tanks feed fuel to the main tanks by means of:
questions.explanation: The auxiliary tanks feed the main wing tanks by transfer ejector pumps.

questions.question: The tail tank system consists of:

options.C: Tail cone tank plus left and right saddle tanks

questions.explanation: The tail tank system consists of a tail cone tank and two saddle tanks.

questions.question: The three tail tanks share a common:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the three tail tanks share a common sump/manifold.

questions.question: The tail tanks may be:

options.D: Refueled from the overwing caps only

questions.explanation: The PTG states the tail tanks may not be gravity refueled.

questions.question: During normal operation, tail tank fuel is transferred to the auxiliary tank system by:
questions.explanation: The PTG states electrically operated tail transfer pumps transfer fuel from the tail tank system to the auxiliary tank system.

questions.question: Tail tank fuel transfer is scheduled and monitored by the:
questions.explanation: The FSCU schedules and monitors tail tank fuel transfer.

questions.question: All tail tank fuel may be dumped through a dump mast located at the:
questions.explanation: The PTG states all tail tank fuel may be dumped through a dump mast located at the sump/manifold of the tail tank system.

questions.question: Water drains are provided for:
questions.explanation: The PTG states water drains are provided for every tank.

questions.question: All wing and auxiliary tanks are vented through:
questions.explanation: Two wing mid-span NACA scoops vent all wing and auxiliary tanks.

questions.question: The tail tanks are vented:

options.A: Through the same system as the wing tanks
options.B: Through a dedicated common vent line

questions.explanation: The tail tanks are vented through their own dedicated system via a common vent line.

questions.question: Ram air in the vent system is used to provide:

options.B: A slight positive pressure during flight

questions.explanation: Ram air is used to provide a slight positive pressure during flight.

questions.question: How many manually testable vent relief valves are provided for main and auxiliary tanks?
questions.explanation: There are two under the wings near the tips and a third beneath the center of the auxiliary tank.

questions.question: If the tail tank relief valve has opened (popped), it indicates:
questions.explanation: The PTG cautions that an opened tail tank relief valve indicates an overpressure condition in the tail tank system.

questions.question: Fuel quantity is measured through a network of:
questions.explanation: The FSCU measures fuel quantity through 17 DC-type capacitance probes.

questions.question: To correct fuel quantity for aircraft attitude, the FSCU receives attitude information from:
questions.explanation: The FSCU receives attitude information from IRS 1 and IRS 2.

questions.question: Fuel quantity information is displayed on the:
questions.explanation: Fuel quantity is displayed on the EICAS primary page.

questions.question: During normal fuel management, fuel is burned from the main tanks until they reach approximately:
questions.explanation: Fuel is burned from the main tanks until they reach 93% of their capacity.

questions.question: After the main tanks reach 93% capacity, fuel transfer commences from the:

options.B: Auxiliary tanks to the main tanks
options.C: Main tanks to the auxiliary tanks
options.D: Collector tanks to the tail tank

questions.explanation: After the main tanks reach 93%, fuel transfer starts from the auxiliary tanks to the main tanks.

questions.question: Main tank fuel transfer is regulated by:

options.B: Float valves in the main tanks

questions.explanation: Float valves in the main tanks regulate transfer and maintain main tanks at 93% capacity.

questions.question: The APU fuel feed system includes an electric boost pump located in the:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the APU fuel feed system includes an electric boost pump in the right main tank.

questions.question: The engine fuel feed system controls the flow of fuel from the main tanks to the:
questions.explanation: The engine fuel feed system controls the flow of fuel from the main fuel tanks to the engines.

questions.question: Two DC electric boost pumps are primarily available for:

options.B: Engine starting and backup fuel feed

questions.explanation: The two DC electric boost pumps provide standby feed for engine starting and as backup fuel feed.

questions.question: The left boost pump is powered by the:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the left boost pump is powered by the DC Battery Bus.

questions.question: The right boost pump is powered by:
questions.explanation: The right boost pump is powered by DC Bus 2.

questions.question: During normal operation, the only pilot action required for the boost pumps is to:

options.A: Turn them ON for all phases of flight
options.B: Arm both before engine start and disarm them after shutdown
options.C: Use only one pump at a time
options.D: Select them ON whenever fuel imbalance occurs

questions.explanation: The PTG says both BOOST PUMP switches are armed prior to start and disarmed after shutdown.

questions.question: If both BOOST PUMP switches are armed, the boost pumps activate when:

options.B: Low pressure is sensed at either main ejector pressure switch

questions.explanation: When both are armed, either low-pressure signal at the main ejector pressure switches will activate the pumps.

questions.question: If a boost pump has activated, sufficient output pressure is indicated by the green:
questions.explanation: Sufficient pump output pressure is indicated by the green ON legend.

questions.question: Insufficient boost pump output pressure is indicated by the amber:
questions.explanation: Insufficient output pressure is indicated by the amber INOP legend.

questions.question: The engine-driven fuel pump supplies high-pressure fuel to the engine FCU and to the:
questions.explanation: The engine-driven pump supplies the FCU and the motive flow system.

questions.question: The engine-driven fuel pump is located on the:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the engine-driven pump is on the engine accessory gearbox.

questions.question: Motive flow is:

options.A: Fuel transfer from tail to auxiliary only
options.B: The continuous movement of fuel under pressure through the feed lines

questions.explanation: Motive flow is defined as the continuous movement of fuel under pressure through the fuel feed lines.

questions.question: Ejector pumps operate on the principle of the:
questions.explanation: The PTG states ejector pumps operate on the same principle as the venturi tube.

questions.question: The main ejector pumps are located:

options.B: On top of their respective collector tanks

questions.explanation: The left and right main ejector pumps are located on top of their respective collector tanks.

questions.question: The scavenge ejector pumps are located:

options.A: At the lowest inboard point of each main tank
options.C: At the top of the collector tanks
options.D: In the auxiliary tank center section

questions.explanation: The left and right scavenge ejector pumps are at the lowest inboard point of each main tank.

questions.question: The transfer ejector pumps are located:

options.A: At the lowest point of the center auxiliary tank

questions.explanation: The transfer ejectors are located at the lowest point of the center auxiliary tank.

questions.question: The motive flow supply for the transfer ejectors is derived from the:
questions.explanation: The transfer ejectors derive motive flow from the on-side main ejector output.

questions.question: The engine fuel shutoff valves are installed:
questions.explanation: Each engine fuel shutoff valve is installed on the engine fuel feed line ahead of the engine-driven pump.

questions.question: Pressing the L(R) ENG FIRE PUSH switch/light causes the engine fuel shutoff valve to:
questions.explanation: The engine fuel SOV closes when the ENG FIRE PUSH switch/light is pressed.

questions.question: A successful closing of the engine fuel shutoff valve is indicated by which advisory message?
questions.explanation: Successful closing is indicated by the L(R) ENG SOV CLOSED advisory.

questions.question: A clogged engine fuel filter is indicated by the:
questions.explanation: Impending bypass (filter clogged) is indicated by the L(R) FUEL FILTER caution EICAS message.

questions.question: Wash filters are installed along the motive flow lines to protect the:
questions.explanation: The wash filter channels ice crystals to the larger main ejector and protects the smaller scavenge ejector from clogging.

questions.question: The FSCU is a two-channel computer located in the:
questions.explanation: The FSCU is located in the underfloor avionic/electronic bay.

questions.question: Which FSCU channel is the primary controller?

options.D: Whichever is selected by the crew

questions.explanation: Channel 1 is the primary controller and channel 2 automatically replaces it on failure.

questions.question: Tail tank transfer pumps normally transfer fuel at a constant rate of approximately:
questions.explanation: The PTG gives a normal transfer rate of 1800 pounds per hour (or 900 lb/hr if SB 604-28-007 is not installed).

questions.question: If rotor-burst protection shroud pressure is low, the amber AUTO INHIB light illuminates and which EICAS caution is displayed?
questions.explanation: Low shroud pressure inhibits automatic transfer and displays AUTO TAIL XFER INHIB.

questions.question: Normal automatic tail tank transfer is inhibited while the aircraft is:
questions.explanation: The PTG notes that normal automatic tail tank transfer is inhibited on the ground with WOW sensed.

questions.question: Fuel dumping from the tail tank system must be carried out with flaps at:
questions.explanation: The PTG explicitly states fuel dumping must be carried out with the flaps at 0°.

questions.question: The Challenger 604 flight controls are actuated by crew or autopilot inputs via:

options.D: Direct hydraulic linkage without mechanical input

questions.explanation: The flight control surfaces are actuated by crew or autopilot inputs via mechanical connections to hydraulic Power Control Units (PCUs).

questions.question: Artificial control loading is provided at the:

options.B: Individual control columns, control wheels, and rudder pedals

questions.explanation: Artificial control loading is provided at the individual control columns, control wheels and rudder pedals.

questions.question: The primary flight controls are:
questions.explanation: The primary flight controls are the ailerons, elevators, and rudder.

questions.question: The secondary flight controls include trim systems, flaps, flight spoilers, ground spoilers, and the:
questions.explanation: The stall protection system is considered part of the secondary flight control system.

questions.question: Each aileron is powered by:
questions.explanation: Each aileron is powered by two hydraulic systems.

questions.question: Each elevator is powered by:
questions.explanation: Each elevator is powered by two hydraulic systems.

questions.question: The rudder is powered by:
questions.explanation: The rudder is powered by all three hydraulic systems.

questions.question: In total electrical failure or double-engine failure, hydraulic power remains available to the flight controls because:

options.B: ACMP 3B can be energized directly from the ADG
options.C: The flaps drive the controls mechanically
options.D: The rudder trim actuator powers all controls

questions.explanation: ACMP 3B can be electrically energized directly from the Air-Driven Generator (ADG), ensuring hydraulic power remains available to the flight controls.

questions.question: Primary flight control position indications are shown on the:
questions.explanation: Position indicators are located on the EICAS Flight Controls synoptic page.

questions.question: The flight control system is designed so that loss of one hydraulic system will:

options.B: Not prevent operation of any control surface

questions.explanation: Loss of one hydraulic system will not prevent operation of any control surface.

questions.question: Should two hydraulic systems be lost, the remaining hydraulic system is sufficient to:
questions.explanation: The remaining hydraulic system is sufficient to maintain safe aircraft control.

questions.question: When parked and the hydraulic systems are unpressurized, rapid control surface movement is prevented by built-in valves in the:
questions.explanation: Built-in valves in the aileron, elevator, and rudder PCUs provide gust damping.

questions.question: Lateral control is provided by the:
questions.explanation: Lateral (roll) control is provided by the ailerons.

questions.question: The pilot's side aileron control circuit normally controls the:
questions.explanation: The pilot’s side controls the left aileron and the copilot’s side controls the right aileron.

questions.question: Each aileron uses how many PCUs?
questions.explanation: Two actuators are used for each aileron.

questions.question: The left aileron PCUs use hydraulic pressure from systems:
questions.explanation: The left aileron PCUs use hydraulic systems 1 and 3.

questions.question: The right aileron PCUs use hydraulic pressure from systems:
questions.explanation: The right aileron PCUs use hydraulic systems 2 and 3.

questions.question: In the event of a control cable jam or PCU malfunction in roll control, the left and right aileron control circuits can be isolated by pulling the:
questions.explanation: The ROLL DISCONNECT handle on the center pedestal isolates the left and right aileron control circuits.

questions.question: After a roll disconnect, the operable circuit provides roll control at a:
questions.explanation: The aileron on the operable circuit provides roll control at a reduced roll rate.

questions.question: If an aileron PCU valve jams, the EICAS Flight Controls synoptic page shows:

options.B: An amber half-circle above the aileron outline
options.C: A red box around the aileron
options.D: A white arrow at maximum deflection

questions.explanation: A PCU valve jam symbol is shown as an amber half-circle above the aileron outline.

questions.question: An aileron PCU malfunction will also produce which EICAS caution message?
questions.explanation: A jammed or malfunctioning aileron PCU produces the AILERON PCU caution message.

questions.question: Each aileron PCU includes shear rivets to provide protection against:
questions.explanation: Two sets of shear rivets on each aileron PCU provide protection against internal PCU linkage jams.

questions.question: A flutter damper is installed on each:
questions.explanation: A flutter damper is installed on each aileron.

questions.question: Longitudinal control is provided by elevators and supplemented by the:
questions.explanation: Pitch control is provided by elevators and supplemented by a movable horizontal stabilizer.

questions.question: The pilot's control column normally controls the:
questions.explanation: The pilot’s control column controls the left elevator and the copilot’s control column controls the right elevator.

questions.question: The left elevator PCUs use hydraulic systems:
questions.explanation: The left elevator PCUs use hydraulic systems 1 and 3.

questions.question: The right elevator PCUs use hydraulic systems:
questions.explanation: The right elevator PCUs use hydraulic systems 2 and 3.

questions.question: An ELEVATOR SPLIT caution appears when the difference between left and right elevator position is greater than:
questions.explanation: ELEVATOR SPLIT appears if a differential greater than 5° exists between the elevator surfaces.

questions.question: In the event of a control cable jam or elevator system malfunction, the left and right elevator control circuits can be isolated by pulling the:
questions.explanation: The PITCH DISC handle isolates the left and right elevator control circuits.

questions.question: After a pitch disconnect, the aircraft retains:

options.A: Normal pitch authority through both elevators
options.B: Reduced pitch authority through one elevator
options.C: No pitch authority until trim is used

questions.explanation: Pitch disconnect provides reduced pitch authority through the one elevator on the operable circuit.

questions.question: Elevator PCU jam protection is provided by:
questions.explanation: Elevator PCU jam or failure can be overcome by integrated jam-tolerant input rods.

questions.question: How many flutter dampers are installed on each elevator?
questions.explanation: Two flutter dampers are installed on each elevator.

questions.question: Yaw control is provided by a hydraulically actuated:
questions.explanation: Yaw control is provided by a hydraulically actuated rudder.

questions.question: The rudder has how many independent PCUs?
questions.explanation: Two separate mechanical control paths are provided from the rudder pedals to three independent PCUs.

questions.question: Both rudder pedal control systems include an anti-jam:
questions.explanation: Both rudder pedal control systems have an anti-jam breakout mechanism.

questions.question: The two-channel yaw damper system supplies small rudder corrections in response to reference signals from the:
questions.explanation: The yaw damper uses reference signals from the IRUs through the FCCs.

questions.question: Pitch trim is accomplished by varying the angle of incidence of the:
questions.explanation: Pitch trim is accomplished by varying the angle of incidence of the horizontal stabilizer.

questions.question: The horizontal stabilizer trim range is approximately:
questions.explanation: The HSTCU commands the stabilizer through a range of 0° to -9°.

questions.question: Normally, with both stabilizer trim channels engaged:

options.A: CH 2 is in command and CH 1 is standby
options.C: CH 1 is in command and CH 2 is standby
options.D: Neither channel is active until autopilot is engaged

questions.explanation: With both channels engaged, CH 1 is in command and CH 2 is in standby.

questions.question: If one stabilizer trim channel fails, which EICAS status message appears?

options.B: STAB CH 1 INOP or STAB CH 2 INOP

questions.explanation: A single-channel failure produces STAB CH 1 INOP or STAB CH 2 INOP.

questions.question: If both stabilizer trim channels fail, the EICAS message is:
questions.explanation: If both channels fail, the STAB TRIM caution appears and stabilizer trim becomes inoperative.

questions.question: The normal takeoff trim setting is indicated by a green band approximately between:
questions.explanation: The normal takeoff trim setting is shown as a green band from about -3° to -6°.

questions.question: If stabilizer trim is not set in the green takeoff range with the aircraft in takeoff mode, which warning appears?
questions.explanation: CONFIG STAB appears, accompanied by the aural warning 'CONFIG TRIM.'

questions.question: The highest priority in stabilizer trim command is:
questions.explanation: Pilot manual trim is highest in the order of priority.

questions.question: Pilot or copilot manual stabilizer trim moves at approximately:
questions.explanation: Manual trim rate is 0.5°/sec.

questions.question: Autopilot stabilizer trim normally moves at:
questions.explanation: Autopilot trim rate is 0.1°/sec, increasing to 0.5°/sec during flap transition with autopilot engaged.

questions.question: Mach trim command rate is approximately:
questions.explanation: Mach trim is lowest priority and moves between 0.03° and 0.06°/sec.

questions.question: If the horizontal stabilizer is in motion at more than 0.3°/sec for more than 3 seconds, a:
questions.explanation: A clacker ('drumroll') sounds to alert the pilot of a possible horizontal stabilizer trim runaway.

questions.question: In the event of a runaway stabilizer trim, both channels can be disconnected by pressing the:

options.C: STAB TRIM DISC button on either outboard control wheel horn

questions.explanation: The STAB TRIM DISC button disconnects both trim channels.

questions.question: The Mach Trim system becomes active when hand-flying at speeds greater than:
questions.explanation: Mach trim schedules stabilizer movement when hand-flying at speeds greater than Mach 0.4.

questions.question: Aileron trim is activated by:

options.B: Moving one switch left or right
options.C: Actuation of both AIL TRIM switches

questions.explanation: Both aileron trim switches must be actuated to provide arming and directional signals.

questions.question: Actuation of aileron trim causes the control wheels to:

options.B: Deflect in the direction of trim input

questions.explanation: Aileron trim causes the control wheels to deflect in the direction of trim input.

questions.question: Rudder trim is accomplished by:

options.D: Using the yaw damper engage switch

questions.explanation: Rudder trim is accomplished by turning the RUD TRIM switch.

questions.question: Actuation of rudder trim causes rudder pedal deflection:
questions.explanation: Rudder trim does not cause rudder pedal deflection.

questions.question: The flaps may be set to which positions?
questions.explanation: The flap positions are 0, 20, 30, and 45 degrees.

questions.question: If a single flap motor overheats, the remaining motor can operate the flaps at:
questions.explanation: A single flap motor overheat leaves the remaining motor to operate the flaps at half speed.

questions.question: A flap asymmetry greater than approximately how much will generate a FLAPS FAIL caution?
questions.explanation: A complete flap failure, including asymmetry greater than 2.75°, generates FLAPS FAIL.

questions.question: If the flaps are not set at 20° with the aircraft in takeoff mode, the warning is:
questions.explanation: CONFIG FLAPS warning and aural warning are generated if takeoff flap setting is not 20°.

questions.question: Each flight spoiler panel is hydraulically operated by two identical PCUs powered by hydraulic systems:
questions.explanation: Flight spoilers are hydraulically operated by two PCUs powered by hydraulic systems 1 and 2.

questions.question: The stick shaker is always armed, while the STALL PROT PUSHER switches affect only the:
questions.explanation: The stick shaker is always armed; the STALL PROT PUSHER switches affect only the stick pusher function.

questions.question: The Challenger 604 landing gear is arranged in a standard:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the landing gear is arranged in a standard retractable tricycle configuration.

questions.question: Hydraulic system 3 powers:

options.A: Landing gear extension/retraction, nosewheel steering, and brakes

questions.explanation: The PTG states hydraulic system 3 powers landing gear extension and retraction, nosewheel steering, and brakes.

questions.question: The main landing gear retracts:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the MLG retracts inward toward the centerline of the fuselage.

questions.question: The nose landing gear is a:
questions.explanation: The PTG describes the nose gear as a forward retracting assembly.

questions.question: The normal landing gear extension/retraction sequence is electrically controlled by the:
questions.explanation: The proximity sensing electronic unit (PSEU) electrically controls normal landing gear sequencing.

questions.question: Each main landing gear assembly is a:
questions.explanation: The PTG states each MLG assembly is a trailing link-type assembly.

questions.question: Each main gear assembly includes all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: A steering actuator is part of the nose gear, not the main gear.

questions.question: Each main wheel bin contains:
questions.explanation: Each wheel bin contains a single-loop overheat detection wire.

questions.question: The MLG is held in the up-and-locked position by:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the MLG is held up and locked by mechanical uplocks.

questions.question: With the MLG extended, downlock is provided by:

options.B: internal locking mechanisms within the MLG actuators

questions.explanation: With the gear extended, internal locking mechanisms within the MLG actuators lock the gear down.

questions.question: The MLG doors are:

options.A: hydraulically actuated separately from the MLG strut
options.B: mechanically attached to each MLG strut

questions.explanation: The MLG doors are mechanically attached to each MLG strut.

questions.question: Each main landing gear assembly has how many WOW proximity sensors?
questions.explanation: Two WOW proximity sensors are incorporated in each main gear assembly.

questions.question: The nose landing gear includes all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: MLG downlock assist actuators are part of the main gear system, not the NLG.

questions.question: The nose gear is held in the up-and-locked position by a:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the nose gear is held up and locked by a mechanical uplock.

questions.question: With the nose gear extended, downlock is provided by an overcenter locking mechanism in the:
questions.explanation: An overcenter locking mechanism in the drag brace holds the nose gear down and locked.

questions.question: When the NLG is down and locked, the forward nose doors:
questions.explanation: When the gear is down and locked, the forward nose doors close to minimize drag.

questions.question: Any nose door fault or position deviation is annunciated by which EICAS warning?
questions.explanation: Any nose door fault or deviation causes the NOSE DOOR OPEN warning and 'NOSE DOOR' aural warning.

questions.question: A maintenance access switch to open/close the nose doors on the ground is installed on the:
questions.explanation: A maintenance access switch is installed on the external service panel.

questions.question: Each nose landing gear assembly has how many WOW proximity sensors?
questions.explanation: Two WOW proximity sensors are incorporated in the nose gear assembly.

questions.question: Normal gear operation is initiated by selecting the LDG GEAR lever:
questions.explanation: Normal retraction or extension is initiated by selecting the LDG GEAR lever UP or DN.

questions.question: Maximum landing gear retraction speed is:
questions.explanation: The PTG states maximum retraction speed is 197 KIAS (0.6 M).

questions.question: During landing gear retraction, the PSEU first releases the:
questions.explanation: During retraction, the PSEU releases the landing gear lever solenoid lock so the lever can be selected UP.

questions.question: During gear retraction, hydraulic pressure from the nose gear up line is directed to the brake control valves to:
questions.explanation: This provides in-flight braking to stop main wheel rotation during retraction.

questions.question: After the retract sequence is complete, the NLG, NLG door, and MLG selector valves are:

options.B: de-energized and hydraulic pressure is removed

questions.explanation: On completion of the retract sequence, selector valves are de-energized and hydraulic pressure is removed.

questions.question: The DN LCK REL button is used when:

options.B: the gear handle solenoid lock or PSEU malfunctions after takeoff
options.D: the parking brake will not release

questions.explanation: DN LCK REL mechanically withdraws the solenoid lock if the solenoid lock or PSEU malfunctions after takeoff.

questions.question: Maximum landing gear extension speed is:
questions.explanation: The PTG states maximum extension speed is 197 KIAS (0.6 M).

questions.question: Maximum airspeed with landing gear extended and locked is:
questions.explanation: The PTG notes the maximum airspeed with gear down and locked is 250 KIAS (0.7 M).

questions.question: If the landing gear position does not match the handle position within 28 seconds, which warning appears?
questions.explanation: If the landing gear position does not match the handle position within 28 seconds, GEAR DISAGREE is displayed.

questions.question: Alternate landing gear extension is accomplished by pulling the red:
questions.explanation: Alternate extension is accomplished by pulling the red landing gear manual release handle.

questions.question: Pulling the manual release handle mechanically releases:

options.C: the nose gear forward door uplock and the three landing gear uplocks
options.D: the parking brake and anti-skid system

questions.explanation: The handle releases the nose gear forward door uplock and the three landing gear uplocks.

questions.question: During alternate extension, hydraulic pressure is removed by:

options.B: manual dump valves operated by the handle

questions.explanation: The handle operates dump valves that remove hydraulic pressure, allowing the gear to free-fall.

questions.question: During alternate landing gear extension, the nose doors:

options.A: close after the nose gear locks
options.D: must be closed with the maintenance switch

questions.explanation: The PTG states the nose doors remain open after alternate extension.

questions.question: During alternate extension, the nose gear is assisted to downlock by airflow and:
questions.explanation: Two downlock assist springs help the nose gear extend and lock.

questions.question: During alternate extension, MLG downlock assist actuators are powered by hydraulic system:
questions.explanation: The MLG downlock assist actuators are powered by hydraulic system 2.

questions.question: To stow the manual release handle after use, you must:

options.B: press the center button and lower the handle
options.C: pull it to the second detent
options.D: cycle hydraulic system 3 OFF then ON

questions.explanation: To reset the handle, the button in the center must be depressed and the handle lowered to the stowed position.

questions.question: If hydraulic system 3 pressure drops below 2100 psi, a priority valve cuts off pressure to the landing gear selector valves to protect:
questions.explanation: The priority valve preserves system 3 pressure for the primary flight controls.

questions.question: The landing gear horn sounds only if the aircraft has been weight off wheels for more than:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the gear horn will sound when the aircraft is weight off wheels for more than two minutes and the conditions are met.

questions.question: The PSEU determines ground mode when:

options.A: all six WOW sensors indicate ground
options.B: any one main gear WOW sensor indicates ground
options.C: any three of the four main gear proximity sensors indicate WOW
options.D: the nose gear WOW sensors indicate ground

questions.explanation: Ground mode is determined when any three of the four main gear proximity sensors indicate WOW.

questions.question: If ground mode conditions are not satisfied, the PSEU is in:
questions.explanation: If ground conditions are not met, the PSEU is in air mode.

questions.question: After takeoff, the PSEU disables:
questions.explanation: After takeoff, the PSEU disables anti-skid and nosewheel steering.

questions.question: At touchdown, the PSEU rearms:
questions.explanation: At touchdown, the PSEU rearms anti-skid and nosewheel steering for landing roll and taxi.

questions.question: Each main gear wheel bin is monitored by:

options.B: an overheat detection unit and heat-sensing loop

questions.explanation: The PTG states each wheel bin is continuously monitored by an overheat detection unit and heat-sensing loop.

questions.question: An actual main gear bay overheat generates which EICAS warning?
questions.explanation: An overheat condition displays MLG BAY OVHT and the aural 'GEAR BAY OVERHEAT.'

questions.question: A shorted overheat-sensing loop in the main gear bay causes which EICAS caution?
questions.explanation: A failure due to a shorted heat-sensing loop displays MLG OVHT FAIL.

questions.question: The nosewheel steering system is controlled electronically using:
questions.explanation: The PTG states nosewheel steering commands are transmitted electronically using steer-by-wire technology.

questions.question: The nosewheel steering system is activated when hydraulic system 3 is pressurized, the nose gear is down and locked, and:

options.A: the aircraft is in air mode
options.B: the PSEU ground mode is satisfied

questions.explanation: Activation requires hydraulic system 3 pressure, nose gear down and locked, and PSEU ground mode.

questions.question: Maximum nosewheel deflection using the steering tiller is:
questions.explanation: Using the steering tiller, nosewheel deflection up to 55° on either side is possible.

questions.question: Maximum nosewheel deflection using rudder pedals is:
questions.explanation: Rudder pedal movement deflects the nosewheel up to 7° either side of center.

questions.question: If the steering angle exceeds 55° while NOSE STEER is armed, the system:

options.A: continues steering to 99° under ECU control
options.B: automatically disables and the nosewheel free-casters

questions.explanation: If 55° is exceeded, the steering system is automatically disabled and the nosewheel free-casters to a maximum of 99°.

questions.question: To reengage nosewheel steering after an over-angle disable, the crew must:

options.B: reduce steering angle below 55° and cycle NOSE STEER OFF then ARMED

questions.explanation: The system cannot be reengaged unless steering angle is less than 55° and the NOSE STEER switch is cycled OFF then ARMED.

questions.question: If the nosewheel steering system fails or is manually disarmed, it reverts to:
questions.explanation: When shut down due to fault or manual disarm, nosewheel steering reverts to free-castering mode.

questions.question: Each main wheel is equipped with:
questions.explanation: Each main wheel is equipped with self-adjusting multidisc carbon brakes.

questions.question: Hydraulic system 3 powers the brakes on the:
questions.explanation: Hydraulic system 3 powers the brakes of the inboard wheels.

questions.question: Hydraulic system 2 powers the brakes on the:
questions.explanation: Hydraulic system 2 powers the brakes of the outboard wheels.

questions.question: Loss of either hydraulic system 2 or 3 results in:

options.B: 50% reduction in braking capability with symmetrical braking remaining
options.C: loss of anti-skid on all wheels

questions.explanation: Loss of either system 2 or 3 causes a 50% reduction in braking, but braking remains symmetrical and anti-skid remains available at the working brakes.

questions.question: With hydraulic systems 2 or 3 failed, accumulators provide pressure for approximately how many brake applications with anti-skid OFF?
questions.explanation: The accumulators provide pressure for approximately six brake applications with anti-skid selected off.

questions.question: The parking brake will hold both inboard and outboard brakes when:

options.A: hydraulic systems 2 and 3 are pressurized and the handle is set

questions.explanation: Both inboard and outboard brakes will hold when systems 2 and 3 are pressurized and the parking brake handle is set.

questions.question: When the hydraulic systems are selected off, the parking brake will hold for a prolonged period on the:
questions.explanation: When hydraulic systems are selected off, only the inboard brakes will hold for a prolonged period.

questions.question: The anti-skid system becomes operational when a 35-knot wheel spin-up signal is generated or:

options.B: a WOW signal is present after a 5-second delay

questions.explanation: The system becomes operational with a 35-knot wheel spin-up signal or a WOW signal after a 5-second delay.

questions.question: The anti-skid system is functional down to a minimum wheel speed of:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the anti-skid system is functional down to a minimum wheel speed of 10 knots.

questions.question: There are how many anti-iced areas on the Challenger 604?
questions.explanation: The PTG lists four anti-iced areas: wing leading edges, engine cowls, windshields/windows, and air data probes.

questions.question: The wing leading edges and engine intake cowls are anti-iced using:
questions.explanation: The PTG states that wing leading edges and engine intake cowls are anti-iced using engine 14th-stage bleed air.

questions.question: The windshields, windows, and air data probes are anti-iced using:
questions.explanation: The PTG states that windshields, windows, and air data probes are anti-iced electrically.

questions.question: Anti-icing of the aircraft tail surfaces is:
questions.explanation: The PTG explicitly states that anti-icing of the aircraft tail surfaces is not required.

questions.question: Wing leading edge anti-icing uses pressure-regulated:
questions.explanation: The wing anti-ice system uses pressure-regulated 14th-stage bleed air.

questions.question: Wing anti-ice bleed air is distributed by ducting and:
questions.explanation: The PTG states wing anti-ice air is distributed by ducting and piccolo tubes.

questions.question: The WING ANTI-ICE switch is located on the:
questions.explanation: The WING ANTI-ICE switch is located on the ANTI-ICE panel.

questions.question: The wing anti-ice system is normally controlled by the WING ANTI-ICE switch through the wing anti-ice controller and:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the switch normally controls the valves via wing-mounted control sensors and the wing anti-ice controller.

questions.question: An isolation valve in the wing anti-ice system allows:

options.A: manual wing crossfeed only on the ground
options.B: both wings to be anti-iced from a single bleed source
options.C: simultaneous use of both thrust reversers and wing anti-ice
options.D: APU bleed supply to the wings in flight

questions.explanation: The 14th-stage isolation valve allows both wings to be anti-iced from a single bleed source through a crossover duct.

questions.question: After heating the wing leading edges, the bleed air is exhausted overboard through:

options.A: louvers beneath each wing leading edge

questions.explanation: The PTG states the wing anti-ice air is exhausted through louvers beneath each wing leading edge.

questions.question: The piccolo tubes in the wing anti-ice system extend:

options.A: only through the inboard wing section
options.B: only through the outboard wing section
options.C: the full length of the leading edges
options.D: from the wing root to mid-span only

questions.explanation: The PTG states the piccolo tubes extend the full length of the wing leading edges.

questions.question: The left and right wing anti-ice valves are:

options.B: electrically controlled, pneumatically operated, pressure-regulating shutoff valves

questions.explanation: The PTG describes the wing anti-ice valves as electrically controlled, pneumatically operated, pressure-regulating shutoff valves.

questions.question: Wing anti-ice valves are spring-loaded to the closed position when:

options.C: no 14th-stage pressure is available or electrical control power is lost

questions.explanation: The PTG states the valves spring closed when no 14th-stage pressure is available or electrical control power is lost.

questions.question: The normal operating mode for the wing anti-ice system is:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the wing anti-ice system is normally operated in NORM mode.

questions.question: A backup operating mode for the wing anti-ice system is:
questions.explanation: The PTG states a backup mode (STBY) exists should NORM mode fail.

questions.question: In NORM mode, the wing anti-ice controller modulates the valves to maintain approximately:
questions.explanation: In NORM mode, the controller maintains constant wing leading edge temperature at 88°C (190°F).

questions.question: The green L HEAT and R HEAT lights indicate:

options.A: the respective wing valve is open
options.B: sufficient heat exists on the respective wing for anti-icing
options.C: the wing controller failed over to STBY

questions.explanation: The PTG states these lights illuminate when the respective wing leading edge is sufficiently heated to prevent ice accumulation.

questions.question: In STBY mode, the wing anti-ice controller is:

options.A: given priority over the thermal switch
options.C: used to control the right wing only

questions.explanation: The PTG states the wing anti-ice controller is bypassed in STBY mode.

questions.question: In STBY mode, the wing anti-ice valves are controlled by the:
questions.explanation: In STBY mode, both wing anti-ice valves are independently controlled by their respective standby thermal switch.

questions.question: In STBY mode, the wing anti-ice valves cycle open and closed between approximately:
questions.explanation: In STBY mode, the valves cycle to maintain wing temperature between 49°C (120°F) and 82°C (180°F).

questions.question: A wing leading edge temperature of 130°C (265°F) or greater causes which EICAS warning?
questions.explanation: WING OVHT appears when a wing leading edge reaches 130°C (265°F) or greater.

questions.question: The aural warning associated with wing overheat is:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the WING OVHT warning is accompanied by the voice warning 'WING OVERHEAT.'

questions.question: The 14th-stage isolation valve for the wing system is normally:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the 14th-stage isolation valve is normally closed during wing anti-icing.

questions.question: The 14th-stage isolation valve may be selected open by the flight crew if:

options.A: both wing valves are operating normally
options.B: a wing anti-ice valve fails or one engine cannot supply 14th-stage bleed air
options.C: the APU is supplying bleed air
options.D: the aircraft is on the ground only

questions.explanation: The PTG states the isolation valve can be opened if a wing anti-ice valve fails or an engine cannot supply 14th-stage bleed air.

questions.question: The 14th-stage isolation valve is controlled by the:

options.C: 14th-stage ISOL switch/light on the BLEED AIR panel

questions.explanation: The PTG states the isolation valve is controlled by the 14th-stage ISOL switch/light on the BLEED AIR panel.

questions.question: Valve status for the 14th-stage isolation valve is indicated by a switch/light annunciation of:
questions.explanation: The PTG states valve status is indicated by an OPEN annunciation.

questions.question: The 14th-stage isolation valve is spring-loaded:

options.A: open when no bleed air is available
options.B: closed when no bleed air or electrical control power is lost

questions.explanation: The PTG states the isolation valve is spring-loaded closed when no 14th-stage bleed air is available or electrical power is lost.

questions.question: Wing anti-ice is automatically disabled during thrust reverser operation because:

options.A: the wing anti-ice controller cannot operate in reverse thrust
options.B: 14th-stage bleed air cannot simultaneously supply both thrust reverser and wing anti-ice demands
options.C: the PSEU inhibits wing anti-ice on the ground

questions.explanation: The PTG states 14th-stage bleed air cannot simultaneously supply the thrust reverser and wing anti-ice system.

questions.question: During thrust reverser operation, wing anti-ice EICAS caution messages are:
questions.explanation: The PTG states wing anti-ice caution messages are inhibited during thrust reverser operation.

questions.question: The engine cowl anti-ice system prevents ice formation on the cowl leading edges and the:

options.B: T2 probe at the engine inlet

questions.explanation: The PTG states the cowl anti-ice system protects the cowl leading edges and the T2 probe.

questions.question: Cowl anti-ice uses pressure-regulated:
questions.explanation: The cowl anti-ice system uses pressure-regulated 14th-stage bleed air.

questions.question: After heating the cowl leading edges, cowl anti-ice air is exhausted overboard through:

options.B: a louvered exit at the bottom of the nacelle

questions.explanation: The PTG states cowl anti-ice air is exhausted through a louvered exit at the bottom of the engine nacelle.

questions.question: The L(R) COWL ANTI-ICE switch/lights are located on the:
questions.explanation: The cowl anti-ice switch/lights are located on the ANTI-ICE panel.

questions.question: Cowl anti-ice valves are:

options.B: electrically controlled, pneumatically operated, pressure-regulating shutoff valves

questions.explanation: The PTG describes the cowl anti-ice valves as electrically controlled, pneumatically operated, pressure-regulating shutoff valves.

questions.question: The cowl anti-ice valves are fail-safed to the:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the cowl anti-ice valves are fail-safed to the open position.

questions.question: If electrical power is lost and bleed air is available, the cowl anti-ice valves will:
questions.explanation: The PTG states loss of electrical power causes the cowl anti-ice valves to move open if bleed air is available.

questions.question: Selecting a COWL switch/light to ON commands the respective cowl anti-ice valve to:
questions.explanation: Selecting the COWL switch/light ON commands the corresponding valve to open.

questions.question: When a cowl anti-ice valve opens successfully, the switch/light illuminates as sensed by the:
questions.explanation: The PTG states switch/light illumination is sensed by the pressure switch.

questions.question: With both cowl anti-ice switches selected ON and both cowls pressurized, the advisory message is:
questions.explanation: COWL A/ICE ON indicates both cowl anti-ice switches are on and both cowls have sufficient pressure.

questions.question: If the left cowl anti-ice switch is ON and only the left cowl has sufficient pressure, the advisory message is:
questions.explanation: L COWL A/ICE ON indicates the left cowl switch is on and the left cowl has sufficient pressure.

questions.question: If a cowl anti-ice valve fails to open with the switch selected ON, the EICAS message is:
questions.explanation: L (R) COWL A/ICE indicates insufficient pressure when the valve fails to open.

questions.question: If a cowl anti-ice valve remains open when deselected, the EICAS message is also:
questions.explanation: The same L (R) COWL A/ICE caution appears if the valve fails to close and pressure remains in the system.

questions.question: Pressure relief valves in the cowl anti-ice system provide overpressure protection in the event of:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the relief valves protect the system if the cowl anti-ice valve malfunctions.

questions.question: When a cowl anti-ice pressure relief valve extends, it can be seen:

options.D: from the cockpit by inspection mirror

questions.explanation: The PTG states the extended relief valve plunger is visible from the ground during external walkaround.

questions.question: The cowl anti-ice system is automatically disabled during thrust reverser operation because:

options.A: cowl ducts cannot withstand reverse flow
options.B: 14th-stage bleed air cannot simultaneously supply both systems

questions.explanation: The PTG states 14th-stage bleed air cannot simultaneously meet thrust reverser and cowl anti-ice demands.

questions.question: The windshield/window anti-ice system provides dual-temperature heating on the windshield and:

options.A: dual-temperature heating on the side windows
options.B: single-temperature heating on the side windows
options.C: no heating on the side windows
options.D: bleed air heating on the side windows

questions.explanation: The PTG states the system provides LOW and HI on the windshields and LOW only on the side windows.

questions.question: With WSHLD/WIND selected HI, the windshield is heated to approximately:
questions.explanation: HI provides windshield heat to 58°C (137°F).

questions.question: With WSHLD/WIND selected LOW, the windshield and side window are heated to approximately:
questions.explanation: LOW heat level is 41°C (106°F) for the corresponding windshield and side window.

questions.question: Moving a WSHLD/WIND switch to OFF/RESET will:

options.B: de-energize the windshield/window and reset the controller
options.C: test the side window heat only

questions.explanation: OFF/RESET removes heat and resets the corresponding temperature controller.

questions.question: The windshield/window TEST switch performs a complete test only when both WSHLD/WIND switches are in:
questions.explanation: The PTG notes both switches must be in HI for a complete windshield/window heat test.

questions.question: With both WSHLD/WIND switches in LOW, pressing TEST checks:

options.A: both windshield and window heat systems
options.C: only the side window heat systems

questions.explanation: With switches in LOW, only the side window heat system is tested.

questions.question: A successful windshield/window heat test is indicated by advisory messages such as:

options.A: L WSHLD HEAT OK and L WINDOW HEAT OK

questions.explanation: Successful testing produces advisory messages for the respective windshield and window heat systems.

questions.question: The L PROBES switch controls the left pitot-static probe, standby pitot, left AOA vane, and:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the L PROBES switch controls the left static port in addition to the other left-side probes.

questions.question: The R PROBES switch controls the right pitot-static probe, TAT probe, right AOA vane, right static port, and:
questions.explanation: The R PROBES switch also controls the auxiliary angle-of-attack probe.

questions.question: Electrical power is applied to the selected probe heaters when the respective PROBES switch is ON and:

options.B: any one engine generator is on-line or the passenger door is locked
options.C: the APU generator is on-line only

questions.explanation: The PTG states heater activation depends on switch position plus any one engine generator on-line or the PAX door locked.

questions.question: If an ADSHC (Air Data Sensor Heater Controller) loses control power, the connected probes will:

options.D: be powered from the opposite ADSHC only

questions.explanation: The PTG states probe heat is fail-safe ON if an ADSHC loses control power.

questions.question: The ice detection system consists of:

options.A: one central detector and one monitor
options.D: one detector tied directly to the wing anti-ice controller

questions.explanation: The PTG states the system consists of two independent ice detector/microprocessors.

questions.question: Each ice detector probe is electrically heated for how long during a deicing cycle?
questions.explanation: During probe deicing, the detector is heated for 5 seconds followed by 55 seconds unheated.

questions.question: When icing is detected and wing and/or cowl anti-ice are not selected on, the EICAS message is:
questions.explanation: The PTG states ICE appears when icing is detected and wing and/or cowl anti-ice are not selected on.

questions.question: If both ice detector channels fail, the EICAS message is:
questions.explanation: The PTG states ICE DETECTORS indicates both ice detector channels have failed.

questions.question: The CL-604 fire protection system is divided into:

options.B: Two subsystems: fire/overheat detection and fire extinguishing

questions.explanation: The PTG states the fire protection system is divided into two separate subsystems: fire and overheat detection, and fire extinguishing.

questions.question: Fire and overheat detection is provided in:

options.A: Engines, jet pipe and pylons, APU, and main landing gear

questions.explanation: The PTG lists engines, jet pipe and pylons, APU, and main landing gear as protected by fire/overheat detection.

questions.question: Smoke detection is provided in the:
questions.explanation: The PTG states smoke detection is provided in the baggage compartment and the lavatory.

questions.question: The fire extinguishing system protects:

options.C: Engine Zone A and the APU enclosure
options.D: Engine Zone B and MLG bays

questions.explanation: The PTG states extinguishing agent is delivered to engine Zone A and the APU enclosure.

questions.question: Portable fire extinguishers are provided in the:
questions.explanation: Portable fire extinguishers are provided in both the cockpit and cabin.

questions.question: Engine fire/overheat protection is divided into:
questions.explanation: The PTG states engine fire/overheat protection is divided into Zone A and Zone B.

questions.question: Engine Zone A includes:

options.A: Jet pipe and pylon area only
options.B: The combustion area of the engine

questions.explanation: Zone A includes the combustion area of the engine.

questions.question: Engine Zone B consists of the:
questions.explanation: Zone B consists of the jet pipe and pylon area.

questions.question: Fire extinguishing is provided for engine:
questions.explanation: The PTG explicitly states only Zone A can receive extinguishing agent.

questions.question: Each engine fire zone is protected by:
questions.explanation: Each engine zone uses a single continuous heat-sensing element connected to its fire detection control unit.

questions.question: The heat-sensing element is basically a:
questions.explanation: The PTG describes each sensing element as a coaxial cable.

questions.question: As temperature increases, the resistance of the sensing element insulating material:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the resistance of the insulating material decreases with increasing temperature.

questions.question: The Zone A sensing element is used to detect an overheat/fire condition in the:
questions.explanation: Zone A detects overheat/fire in the combustion area.

questions.question: The Zone B sensing element is used to detect an overheat/fire condition in the:

options.A: Jet pipe/pylon area and exhaust nozzle area

questions.explanation: Zone B covers the area where electrical harnesses and plumbing go through the pylon, plus the exhaust nozzle area.

questions.question: Each heat-sensing element has its own:
questions.explanation: Each heat-sensing element has its own fire detection control unit which monitors loop resistance.

questions.question: If engine Zone A detects fire/overheat, which EICAS warning is displayed?
questions.explanation: Zone A warning produces L (R) ENG FIRE.

questions.question: If engine Zone A detects fire/overheat, the MASTER WARNING switch/lights will:
questions.explanation: Zone A fire detection causes MASTER WARNING switch/lights to flash.

questions.question: If engine Zone A detects fire/overheat, which aural warning sounds?
questions.explanation: Engine nacelle fire in Zone A triggers the fire bell.

questions.question: If engine Zone B detects overheat, which EICAS warning is displayed?
questions.explanation: Zone B detection causes the L (R) JETPIPE OVHT warning.

questions.question: The aural warning for engine Zone B overheat is:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the Zone B aural warning is 'JETPIPE OVERHEAT.'

questions.question: Aural fire/overheat warnings can be silenced by pressing:
questions.explanation: The PTG states either MASTER WARNING switch/light silences the aural warning.

questions.question: After silencing the aural warning, the EICAS warning and FIRE PUSH light remain until:
questions.explanation: The message and FIRE PUSH indication remain until the fire/overheat condition is corrected.

questions.question: The fire detection control units can discriminate between:

options.B: An actual fire/overheat and a shorted loop

questions.explanation: The PTG states the control units can distinguish a true fire/overheat from a shorted sensing loop.

questions.question: A failed engine Zone A loop produces which caution?
questions.explanation: A failed engine fire detection loop produces L (R) FIRE FAIL.

questions.question: A failed engine Zone B loop produces which caution?
questions.explanation: A failed jet pipe/pylon loop produces L (R) JETPIPE OVHT FAIL.

questions.question: How many engine fire extinguisher bottles are installed?
questions.explanation: The engine extinguishing system consists of two fire extinguisher bottles.

questions.question: The engine fire bottles are located:

options.B: In the aft equipment bay under the APU enclosure

questions.explanation: The PTG states both engine Firex bottles are installed in the aft equipment bay under the APU enclosure.

questions.question: The engine fire bottles are charged with:
questions.explanation: The engine Firex bottles are charged with Halon 1301.

questions.question: The engine fire bottles are pressurized with dry nitrogen to approximately:
questions.explanation: The PTG gives bottle charge pressure as 600 to 625 psi.

questions.question: The engine fire bottle thermal discharge port opens if bottle pressure exceeds approximately:
questions.explanation: The thermal discharge port provides overpressure relief if bottle pressure exceeds 1400 psi.

questions.question: Each engine fire bottle has:
questions.explanation: Each bottle is equipped with a left and right squib.

questions.question: The discharge lines of the engine fire bottles are interconnected to allow:

options.B: Both bottles to discharge into one engine if needed
options.C: Automatic discharge into both engines simultaneously

questions.explanation: The PTG states the discharge lines are interconnected to allow discharge of both bottles into one engine if needed.

questions.question: Pressing LH or RH ENG FIRE PUSH will:

options.B: Arm both engine bottles for discharge

questions.explanation: Pressing ENG FIRE PUSH arms the bottle squibs and illuminates BOTTLE 1 and 2 ARMED.

questions.question: Pressing an ENG FIRE PUSH switch/light also causes the associated engine generator to:
questions.explanation: The PTG lists engine generator shutdown as one of the actions of ENG FIRE PUSH.

questions.question: Pressing an ENG FIRE PUSH switch/light also closes the associated:

options.A: fuel, hydraulic, and bleed air shutoff valves

questions.explanation: The PTG lists closure of the fuel SOV, hydraulic SOV, and bleed air SOV.

questions.question: Pressing an ENG FIRE PUSH switch/light also disables the associated:
questions.explanation: Associated engine igniters are disabled.

questions.question: When an illuminated BOTTLE 1 or 2 ARMED PUSH TO DISCH switch/light is pressed:
