options.B: The bottle squib fires and Halon is directed to the selected engine nacelle
options.D: The bottle is merely armed again

questions.explanation: Pressing the armed bottle switch fires the squib and discharges Halon into the selected engine nacelle.

questions.question: Which switch position on FIRE DET verifies continuity of sensing elements and bottle charge status?
questions.explanation: Selecting FIRE DET to WARN verifies loop continuity and bottle charge status.

questions.question: Which switch position on FIRE DET verifies the control units can discriminate between a true fire and a shorted loop?
questions.explanation: Selecting FIRE DET to FAIL verifies the ability to discriminate between actual overheat/fire and a shorted loop.

questions.question: The FIREX MONITOR TEST switches provide a means to test:
questions.explanation: The PTG states FIREX MONITOR TEST switches test the respective squib circuitry continuity.

questions.question: The APU fire detection system monitors:

options.B: Only when APU bleed is selected

questions.explanation: The PTG states the APU fire detection system continuously monitors the APU.

questions.question: The APU is protected by:

options.D: One thermal switch and one smoke detector

questions.explanation: The PTG states the APU is protected by one continuous heat-sensing element.

questions.question: An APU fire/overheat condition causes:

options.B: Automatic APU shutdown plus EICAS, aural, and visual warning
options.D: Only bottle arming with no shutdown

questions.explanation: The PTG states the APU will automatically shut down and EICAS, aural, and visual warnings occur.

questions.question: An APU fire warning causes which EICAS warning message?
questions.explanation: The PTG lists APU FIRE as the warning message.

questions.question: An APU fire warning also causes:

options.B: MASTER WARNING flashing, fire bell, and APU FIRE PUSH illumination

questions.explanation: The PTG lists flashing MASTER WARNING, fire bell, and illuminated APU FIRE PUSH.

questions.question: If the APU fire detection loop fails, the EICAS caution is:
questions.explanation: A failed APU fire detector loop produces APU FIRE FAIL.

questions.question: How many APU fire extinguisher bottles are installed?
questions.explanation: The APU fire extinguishing system consists of one bottle.

questions.question: The APU fire bottle is located:

options.B: Adjacent to the APU compartment in the aft equipment bay

questions.explanation: The PTG states the APU bottle is located adjacent to the APU compartment in the aft equipment bay.

questions.question: The APU fire bottle is charged with:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the APU bottle is charged with Halon 1301.

questions.question: The APU fire bottle uses a squib with:
questions.explanation: The APU bottle uses a squib with dual firing cartridges for redundancy.

questions.question: Pressing the APU FIRE PUSH switch/light will:

options.B: Arm the bottle, shut down the APU, close APU fuel/negative-G SOVs and LCV, and shut down the APU generator

questions.explanation: The PTG lists all of these actions for APU FIRE PUSH.

questions.question: Pressing the illuminated APU BOTTLE ARMED PUSH TO DISCH switch/light will:

options.B: Fire the squib and discharge Halon into the APU enclosure

questions.explanation: Pressing the armed APU bottle switch fires the squib and discharges the bottle into the APU enclosure.

questions.question: Baggage compartment fire protection consists of:

options.A: A smoke detector interfacing with EICAS
options.B: An automatic extinguisher and heat loop

questions.explanation: The PTG states baggage compartment fire protection consists of an electronic smoke detector that interfaces with EICAS.

questions.question: If smoke is detected in the baggage compartment, the warning is:

options.A: SMOKE BAGGAGE BAY with MASTER WARNING and 'SMOKE' aural warning

questions.explanation: The PTG lists SMOKE BAGGAGE BAY, flashing MASTER WARNING, and 'SMOKE' aural warning.

questions.question: The baggage smoke detector is tested by pressing and holding the:

options.B: SMK DET test button on the annunciator panel

questions.explanation: The baggage detector is tested with the SMK DET button on the annunciator panel.

questions.question: Lavatory fire protection consists of:

options.A: A smoke detector and an automatic fire extinguisher in the waste container

questions.explanation: The PTG states lavatory fire protection includes an electronic smoke detector and an independent automatic fire extinguisher in the waste container.

questions.question: If smoke is detected in the lavatory, the EICAS message is:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the lavatory smoke warning message is SMOKE TOILET.

questions.question: The lavatory waste container extinguisher releases:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the automatic lavatory extinguisher releases Halon 1211.

questions.question: Optional galley overheat protection gives which EICAS warning and aural warning?
questions.explanation: The optional galley overheat detector gives GALLEY OVHT and a triple attenson.

questions.question: If a fire extinguisher is discharged in the flight compartment, all flight crew must wear oxygen masks with:
questions.explanation: The PTG specifically warns that all flight crew must wear oxygen masks with EMERGENCY selected (100% oxygen) if a fire extinguisher is discharged in the flight compartment.

questions.question: The Flight Instruments chapter includes:

options.B: EFIS, Air Data System, standby instruments, clock, and radio altimeter

questions.explanation: The chapter includes the Electronic Flight Instrument System (EFIS), Air Data System (ADS), standby instruments, clock, and radio altimeter.

questions.question: The Electronic Flight Instrument System (EFIS) displays information on:
questions.explanation: EFIS displays information on four Electronic Flight Displays (EFDs).

questions.question: In the CL-604 EFIS, how many EFDs function as PFDs?
questions.explanation: Two EFDs function as Primary Flight Displays (PFDs).

questions.question: In the CL-604 EFIS, how many EFDs function as MFDs?
questions.explanation: Two EFDs function as Multifunction Displays (MFDs).

questions.question: According to EFIS color conventions, white is used for:

options.C: All scales and on-side FMS sensor data
options.D: Cross-side sensor data and caution information

questions.explanation: White is used for all scales and on-side FMS sensor data.

questions.question: According to EFIS color conventions, green is used for:
questions.explanation: Green is used for on-side sensor data that is not FMS-related.

questions.question: According to EFIS color conventions, yellow is used for:

options.B: Cross-side sensor data and caution information

questions.explanation: Yellow is used for cross-side sensor data and caution information.

questions.question: According to EFIS color conventions, magenta is used for:
questions.explanation: Magenta is used for pilot-selectable data.

questions.question: According to EFIS color conventions, cyan is used for:
questions.explanation: Cyan is used for secondary information.

questions.question: According to EFIS color conventions, red is used for:
questions.explanation: Red is used for warnings and failure flags.

questions.question: The Primary Flight Displays are located:
questions.explanation: The two PFDs are the outboard EFDs of the instrument panel.

questions.question: The PFD presents the flight instruments in a typical:
questions.explanation: The PFD presents the flight instruments in a typical “T” formation.

questions.question: The PFD includes both the ADI and the:
questions.explanation: The PFD includes both the Attitude Director Indicator (ADI) and the Horizontal Situation Indicator (HSI).

questions.question: The PFDs come on automatically upon receiving:
questions.explanation: The PFDs come on automatically upon receiving DC power.

questions.question: Which of the following is displayed on the PFD?
questions.explanation: The PFD displays attitude, heading, airspeed, altitude, vertical speed, flight mode annunciators, nav source/navigation information, and the HSI.

questions.question: The Multifunction Displays are located:
questions.explanation: The MFDs are the displays located inboard of the PFDs.

questions.question: The primary purpose of the MFDs is to present:
questions.explanation: The MFDs present navigation information in pilot-selectable formats.

questions.question: The MFDs also provide reversionary backup displays for the:
questions.explanation: The MFDs also provide reversionary backup displays for the PFD or the EICAS.

questions.question: Which of the following is a selectable MFD page format?
questions.explanation: Selectable MFD formats include HSI, Nav Sector Map, FMS Map, Plan Map, and TCAS.

questions.question: Additional information that can be shown on the MFD includes weather radar, terrain data (if installed), checklist, and:
questions.explanation: Additional MFD information includes weather radar, terrain data, checklist, reversion, and remote text.

questions.question: If defined comparator trip levels are exceeded, which EICAS caution is displayed?
questions.explanation: When comparator trip levels are exceeded, the EFIS COMP MON caution is displayed.

questions.question: Full-time EFIS comparator monitoring is performed for pitch, roll, heading, altitude, and:
questions.explanation: The full-time comparator monitors pitch, roll, heading, altitude, and indicated airspeed.

questions.question: The pitch comparator warning appears as:
questions.explanation: The pitch comparator warning is displayed as “PIT.”

questions.question: The roll comparator warning appears as:
questions.explanation: The roll comparator warning is displayed as “ROL.”

questions.question: The heading comparator warning appears when heading difference exceeds:
questions.explanation: If heading difference is greater than 6°, the “HDG” comparator warning appears.

questions.question: For pitch and roll comparators, the trip threshold en route is:
questions.explanation: Pitch and roll comparator warnings occur above 4° en route.

questions.question: For pitch and roll comparators, the trip threshold during approach is:
questions.explanation: Pitch and roll comparator warnings occur above 3° during approach.

questions.question: The airspeed comparator is enabled only above:
questions.explanation: The IAS comparator is enabled when indicated airspeed is greater than 90 knots.

questions.question: If the airspeed comparator is enabled, the comparator warning appears when the difference exceeds:
questions.explanation: The IAS comparator warning appears if the airspeed difference is greater than 10 knots.

questions.question: Category II comparator monitoring is performed for LOC, G/S, and:
questions.explanation: Category II comparator monitoring covers LOC, G/S, and RA deviations.

questions.question: Category II comparators are enabled if approach mode is selected and the DH is set below:
questions.explanation: Category II comparators are enabled if approach mode is selected and DH is set below 200 feet.

questions.question: For Category II comparison, the on-side radio altitude must be less than:
questions.explanation: The on-side RA must be valid and less than 1000 feet.

questions.question: If the comparator monitor itself fails, which EICAS caution appears?
questions.explanation: If the comparator monitor fails, the amber EFIS COMP MON INOP caution is displayed.

questions.question: If airspeed data becomes invalid on the PFD, it is replaced by a red:
questions.explanation: Invalid airspeed data is replaced with a red IAS annunciation.

questions.question: If barometric altitude data becomes invalid on the PFD, it is replaced by a red:
questions.explanation: Invalid barometric altitude data is replaced with a red ALT annunciation.

questions.question: The Air Data System supplies EFIS with altitude, IAS, VS, TAS, SAT, and:
questions.explanation: The Air Data System supplies altitude, IAS, VS, TAS, SAT, and TAT.

questions.question: The Air Data System also provides computed data to AFCS, TCAS, FDR, IRS, and:
questions.explanation: ADS computed data is also sent to EICAS, among other systems.

questions.question: The Air Data System contains the Pitot-Static System, Temperature Sensing System, ADC system, and:
questions.explanation: The ADS contains Pitot-Static, Temperature Sensing, ADC, and ADRP subsystems.

questions.question: The pitot-static system includes:

options.A: One main pitot-static system and one standby
options.B: Two main pitot-static systems and one standby pitot-static system

questions.explanation: The pitot-static system includes two main pitot-static systems and one standby pitot-static system.

questions.question: The two main pitot-static systems are designated:
questions.explanation: The two main systems are designated P1/S1 and P2/S2.

questions.question: The standby pitot system is designated:
questions.explanation: The standby pitot system is designated P3.

questions.question: The two standby static ports are designated:
questions.explanation: Two S3 standby static ports supply standby static pressure.

questions.question: The standby pitot source P3 supplies pressure to the:
questions.explanation: P3 standby pitot pressure is supplied to the standby airspeed indicator.

questions.question: The standby static ports S3 supply static pressure to the standby airspeed indicator, standby altimeter, and:
questions.explanation: S3 ports supply the CPAM cabin pressure controller, standby ASI, and standby altimeter.

questions.question: Ice protection for all pitot-static probes is provided by:
questions.explanation: All pitot-static probes are electrically heated.

questions.question: The temperature-sensing system receives data from a:
questions.explanation: The temperature-sensing system receives data from a Total Air Temperature (TAT) probe.

questions.question: The ADCs are described as:
questions.explanation: The ADCs are digital, microprocessor-controlled units.

questions.question: Which of the following is calculated by the ADC?
questions.explanation: The ADC calculates pressure altitude, Mach, TAS, IAS/CAS, VS, TAT, SAT, and more.

questions.question: Which of the following is computed by the ADC rather than directly sensed?

options.A: Secondary speed references such as V1, VR, V2, VT

questions.explanation: The ADC computes secondary speed references like V1, VR, V2, and VT.

questions.question: The DCP FORMAT knob outer function selects:
questions.explanation: The FORMAT outer knob selects the desired MFD display format.

questions.question: The DCP RANGE knob inner function is used to:
questions.explanation: The inner RANGE knob varies the display range scale on the MFD.

questions.question: The NAV SOURCE knob can select:

options.A: FMS 1, VOR 1/LOC 1, VOR 2/LOC 2, FMS 2, and FMS 3

questions.explanation: Possible NAV SOURCE selections are FMS 1, VOR 1/LOC 1, VOR 2/LOC 2, FMS 2, and FMS 3.

questions.question: Pushing the center of the NAV SOURCE knob alternately adds or removes:

options.B: Cross-side nav data on MFD HSI and NAV Sector displays

questions.explanation: Pushing the center of the NAV SOURCE knob adds or removes cross-side NAV data on MFD HSI and NAV Sector formats.

questions.question: The single-bar BRG pointer can select which No. 1 bearing source?
questions.explanation: The single-bar bearing pointer can select VOR 1, ADF 1, FMS 1, and FMS 3.

questions.question: The double-bar BRG pointer can select which No. 2 bearing source?
questions.explanation: The double-bar bearing pointer can select VOR 2, ADF 2, FMS 2, and FMS 3.

questions.question: The WX button on the DCP is used to select:
questions.explanation: The WX button selects weather radar and lightning detection depictions.

questions.question: The TERR button on the DCP:

options.B: Selects terrain information and replaces weather radar information

questions.explanation: The TERR button selects terrain information and replaces weather radar information.

questions.question: The ADRP is used to set speed, altitude, and:
questions.explanation: The Air Data Reference Panel is used to set speed, altitude, and barometric pressure values.

questions.question: The ADRP altitude reference section is used to control the display of:
questions.explanation: The altitude reference section controls the Minimum Descent Altitude (MDA) and Decision Height (DH) display.

questions.question: Pressing the RA TEST switch on the ADRP causes the radio altitude display to change to:
questions.explanation: During the RA test, the radio altitude value changes to 50 feet and the rising runway symbol moves accordingly.

questions.question: The Navigation Systems chapter covers independent position determining systems, dependent position determining systems, and the:
questions.explanation: The chapter introduction states it covers independent systems, dependent systems, and the Flight Management System (FMS).

questions.question: Independent position determining systems operate independently of:
questions.explanation: Independent systems establish aircraft position without relying on ground installations or orbital satellites.

questions.question: Which of the following is listed as an independent position determining system?
questions.explanation: The chapter lists IRS, Weather Radar, and EGPWS as independent position determining systems.

questions.question: Which of the following is listed as a dependent position determining system?
questions.explanation: Dependent systems include VHF Nav, ADF, DME, ATC transponder, TCAS II, and GPS.

questions.question: The FMS provides cockpit management, flight management, and:
questions.explanation: The FMS provides cockpit management, flight management, and diagnostic functions.

questions.question: A standard cockpit configuration associated with navigation systems includes two DCPs, two PFDs, two MFDs, two RTUs, two CDUs, and:
questions.explanation: The associated components list includes one Source Selector Panel.

questions.question: The IRS installed in the Challenger 604 is the:
questions.explanation: The PTG identifies the IRS as the Litton LTN-101 Inertial Reference Unit.

questions.question: A standard CL-604 installation consists of how many IRS systems?
questions.explanation: The standard installation consists of IRS 1 and IRS 2, with an optional third IRS available.

questions.question: Each IRU uses three laser gyros and three linear accelerometers to sense aircraft:
questions.explanation: Each IRU uses three laser gyros and three linear accelerometers to sense aircraft attitude and acceleration.

questions.question: After successful alignment, an IRU outputs attitude, heading, inertial position, ground speed, and:
questions.explanation: IRU outputs include wind direction and speed in addition to attitude, heading, position, and speed data.

questions.question: Each IRS receives true airspeed and altitude from the:
questions.explanation: Each IRS normally receives TAS and altitude from the on-side air data system, with automatic cross-side selection upon failure.

questions.question: Each IRU has how many power sources?
questions.explanation: Each IRU has a normal 115V AC source and a backup 28V DC source.

questions.question: Both IRU power sources are required for:
questions.explanation: Either source is sufficient for operation, but both are required for initial startup.

questions.question: The advisory message IRS 1(2)(3) ON BATT indicates the respective IRU is operating on:
questions.explanation: IRS ON BATT indicates the respective system is operating on backup battery/DC power.

questions.question: Each IRS mode selector has which three positions?
questions.explanation: Each MSU selector has OFF, NAV, and ATT positions.

questions.question: With IRS reversionary selectors at NORM, PFD and MFD displays normally receive data from their:
questions.explanation: At NORM, each side normally uses on-side IRS data.

questions.question: In a dual-IRS installation, selecting the IRS reversionary selector to 1 or 2 causes:

options.A: only the selected side to receive that IRS data
options.B: both pilot and copilot displays to receive data from the selected IRU
options.D: the system to enter ATT mode

questions.explanation: In a dual-IRS setup, selecting 1 or 2 feeds both sides from that selected IRS.

questions.question: Normal IRS alignment automatically sequences to NAV mode after approximately:
questions.explanation: With required conditions met, the IRU sequences to NAV mode 7 minutes after selecting NAV.

questions.question: Normal IRS alignment requires the aircraft to be:

options.B: on the ground with no excessive motion detected

questions.explanation: Normal alignment requires the aircraft to be on the ground and stationary without excessive motion.

questions.question: To initialize the IRS, present latitude and longitude must be entered from either FMS CDU via the:
questions.explanation: IRS initialization is completed via the POS INIT page.

questions.question: During IRS initialization, the PFD message shown is:
questions.explanation: During ground alignment, the PFD shows IRS ALIGNING DO NOT TAXI.

questions.question: Rapid IRS realignment is initiated by rotating the MSU selector from NAV to OFF and back to NAV within:
questions.explanation: Rapid realignment occurs when the selector is cycled NAV-OFF-NAV within 5 seconds.

questions.question: Total rapid IRS realignment time is approximately:
questions.explanation: Rapid realignment takes about 32 seconds total.

questions.question: In IRS ATT mode, the system provides only:
questions.explanation: ATT mode provides pitch, roll, and heading outputs only.

questions.question: The recommended method of IRS shutdown is to set the MSU selector to:
questions.explanation: The recommended method is to select OFF on the MSU.

questions.question: After selecting the IRS to OFF, aircraft power should not be removed until at least:
questions.explanation: The PTG recommends keeping power applied for at least 15 seconds after selecting OFF.

questions.question: The weather radar detects wet precipitation and precipitation-related turbulence along the:
questions.explanation: The radar detects wet precipitation and related turbulence along the aircraft flight path.

questions.question: An optional system adds lightning detection to the weather radar displays. It is the:
questions.explanation: An optional Lightning Detection System can be added to the weather radar displays.

questions.question: The weather radar receiver-transmitter antenna scan is:
questions.explanation: The RTA scans ±60° relative to aircraft heading.

questions.question: The selectable radar antenna tilt angle is:
questions.explanation: The selectable antenna tilt is ±15° above and below horizontal.

questions.question: The weather radar RTA is powered by:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the RTA is powered by DC Bus 1.

questions.question: Available weather radar operating modes include OFF, TEST, MAP, WX, WX+T, and:
questions.explanation: The available radar modes include TURB (turbulence only).

questions.question: Selecting sector scan with the SEC button changes the radar scan to:
questions.explanation: Sector scan reduces the azimuth to ±30°.

questions.question: In a dual-WXP installation, pressing XFR transfers complete weather radar control to the:
questions.explanation: In dual-WXP installations, XFR transfers range, mode, gain, and tilt control to the cross-side WXP and DCP.

questions.question: When weather radar transfer is selected, an "X" annunciates on:
questions.explanation: Transfer selection is indicated by an X on both MFD radar mode lines.

questions.question: Normally, the STAB function is selected to automatically stabilize the antenna using attitude input from the:
questions.explanation: Antenna stabilization normally uses attitude input from the IRS.

questions.question: If stabilization is not available, the MFD annunciation shown is:
questions.explanation: USTB indicates stabilization is not available.

questions.question: Selecting automatic tilt is indicated on the MFD tilt readout by:
questions.explanation: An "A" appended to the tilt angle indicates automatic tilt is selected.

questions.question: The DCP WX button cycles weather and lightning display selections in sequence. Which is NOT one of the selections?
questions.explanation: WX cycles through WX, LX, WX/LX, then OFF. TAAD is not part of the weather radar WX button sequence.

questions.question: Available weather radar display ranges are:
questions.explanation: Weather radar ranges are 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200, and 300 NM.

questions.question: In WX+T mode, turbulence detection is operational for ranges:

options.B: less than or equal to 40 NM

questions.explanation: WX+T is operational for ranges less than or equal to 40 NM.

questions.question: If a range greater than 50 NM is selected while in WX+T mode, the weather radar reverts to:
questions.explanation: At greater than 50 NM, WX+T reverts to WX mode.

questions.question: The lightning detection system maps electrical discharge activity around the aircraft out to approximately:
questions.explanation: The optional lightning detection system maps lightning activity out to 200 NM.

questions.question: The EGPWS alerts the crew when the airplane’s flight path and position relative to terrain require:
questions.explanation: EGPWS is designed to alert and warn the crew when terrain-related conditions require immediate attention and action.

questions.question: Mode 1 of the EGPWS is:
questions.explanation: Mode 1 is Excessive Descent Rate.

questions.question: Mode 2 of the EGPWS is:
questions.explanation: Mode 2 is Excessive Terrain Closure Rate.

questions.question: Mode 5 of the EGPWS is:
questions.explanation: Mode 5 is the descent below glideslope deviation alert.

questions.question: Mode 7 of the EGPWS is:
questions.explanation: Mode 7 is Windshear Detection and Alerting.

questions.question: Modes 1 through 6 are installed in:

options.B: all aircraft as the basic GPWS functions

questions.explanation: Modes 1 through 6 are installed in all aircraft as the basic GPWS package.

questions.question: Mode 1 is active when the airplane is less than:
questions.explanation: Mode 1 is active below 2500 feet AGL.

questions.question: Mode 1 alert area penetration generates the aural message:
questions.explanation: Mode 1 alert area triggers the SINKRATE SINKRATE aural alert.

questions.question: Mode 1 warning area penetration generates the aural warning:
questions.explanation: Mode 1 warning area triggers the WHOOP, WHOOP, PULL UP warning.

questions.question: Mode 2 has two submodes identified as:
questions.explanation: The PTG identifies Mode 2A and Mode 2B.

questions.question: Mode 2A is activated when flaps are not in landing position and the aircraft is:
questions.explanation: Mode 2A applies when flaps are not in landing position and the aircraft is not on the glideslope beam.

questions.question: Mode 2B is activated when flaps are in landing configuration or when the aircraft is on an ILS approach with localizer and glideslope deviations less than:
questions.explanation: Mode 2B applies when glideslope and localizer deviations are less than ±2 dots.

questions.question: The VHF Navigation System is a dual-receiver system used for en route navigation and for:
questions.explanation: The VHF Navigation System is used for en route navigation and precision approach and landing.

questions.question: The VOR/LOC receiver frequency range is:
questions.explanation: The VOR/LOC receiver operates from 108.0 through 117.95 MHz.

questions.question: The marker beacon operates on a fixed frequency of:
questions.explanation: The marker beacon operates on a fixed frequency of 75.0 MHz.

questions.question: Each DME is a three-channel transceiver. Channel one is normally tuned:

options.B: manually by the on-side RTU, cross-side RTU, or by the FMS
options.C: only by the audio control panel

questions.explanation: Channel one may be manually tuned by the on-side RTU, cross-side RTU, or by the FMS.

questions.question: GPS is normally the highest-priority sensor used by the FMS integrated navigation solution, with other valid sensors blended as needed.

options.D: Only when DME HOLD is selected

questions.explanation: The FMS normally prioritizes GPS, but blends valid DME/DME, VOR/DME, and IRS data according to predetermined algorithms.

questions.question: The Communications chapter includes the Audio Integrating System, VHF Communication System, HF Communication System, Radio Tuning System, Cockpit Voice Recorder System, Flight Data Recorder System, and:
questions.explanation: The chapter introduction lists Static Dischargers as one of the communications-related systems.

questions.question: The audio integrating system provides an interface between crew microphones, speakers, headphones, and the aircraft’s:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the audio integrating system interfaces with the aircraft’s radio, warning, and recording systems.

questions.question: The audio integrating system also provides an interface between any or all flight crew and:
questions.explanation: The system also interfaces between flight crew and ground crew stations.

questions.question: How many flight crew jack panels are installed?
questions.explanation: There are two flight crew jack panels, one for the pilot and one for the copilot.

questions.question: How many ground crew interphone-jack units are installed?
questions.explanation: The PTG states there are three ground crew interphone-jack units.

questions.question: How many speakers are installed for the audio integrating system?
questions.explanation: Two independent speakers are mounted in the roof of the flight compartment.

questions.question: How many audio control panels are installed?
questions.explanation: The PTG lists two audio control panels.

questions.question: The three ground crew interphone-jack units are installed in the nosewheel well, main avionics bay, and:
questions.explanation: The three locations are nosewheel well, main avionics bay, and aft equipment compartment.

questions.question: The microphone jacks in the nosewheel well and aft equipment compartment are deactivated in flight by the:
questions.explanation: The PTG states these microphone jacks are deactivated in flight by the WOW switch.

questions.question: The flight compartment speakers allow audio monitoring without the use of:
questions.explanation: The speakers allow audio monitoring without headphones.

questions.question: The audio control panels provide controls for selecting and controlling receiver audio from the:
questions.explanation: The audio control panels provide controls for selecting and controlling receiver audio from the COM/NAV systems.

questions.question: The MIC selector switch on the audio control panel has positions for PA, VHF 1/2,HF 1/2, and:
questions.explanation: The MIC selector includes PA, VHF 3, HF 1/2, and INT/SVC.

questions.question: The Public Address (PA) selection is used for:
questions.explanation: PA is identified as flight deck to cabin communication.

questions.question: The emergency/normal selector on the audio panel has positions:
questions.explanation: The switch positions are NORM and EMER.

questions.question: In EMER, the audio system provides:

options.C: RX/TX on VHF, nav audio, and aural warnings on boom and mask microphone paths

questions.explanation: The EMER position services selected VHF RX/TX, nav audio, and aural warnings on boom and mask microphone paths.

questions.question: The R/T-I/C switch on the audio panel is used for:

options.B: radio transmit or hot mic intercom with boom/mask mic

questions.explanation: The R/T-I/C switch is used for boom or mask mic radio transmit or hot mic intercom.

questions.question: The VOICE/BOTH switch on the audio panel selects between:

options.A: voice only or voice plus CW ident

questions.explanation: VOICE selects voice information only, BOTH selects voice and CW ident.

questions.question: The MASK/BOOM switch selects between:

options.B: oxygen mask microphone and boom microphone

questions.explanation: The switch selects the oxygen mask microphone or boom microphone.

questions.question: The CL-604 VHF communication system consists of how many independent transceivers?
questions.explanation: The VHF communication system consists of two independent transceivers: VHF 1 and VHF 2.

questions.question: The VHF transceivers provide AM short-range voice communications in the frequency range of:
questions.explanation: The PTG gives the VHF communication range as 118.00 to 151.975 MHz.

questions.question: VHF frequencies are selectable in increments of:
questions.explanation: The PTG states VHF is selectable in 25 kHz or 8.33 kHz increments.

questions.question: The VHF 1 antenna is located on the:
questions.explanation: The VHF 1 antenna is on the upper side of the fuselage.

questions.question: The VHF 2 antenna is located on the:
questions.explanation: The VHF 2 antenna is on the underside of the fuselage.

questions.question: The Integrated Avionics Processor System (IAPS) primarily:

options.B: reads avionics buses and distributes data to LRUs

questions.explanation: The IAPS reads several avionics buses and distributes data to LRUs requiring the information.

questions.question: A VHF transceiver is normally tuned by the:
questions.explanation: The VHF transceiver is normally tuned by the on-side RTU.

questions.question: A VHF transceiver may also be tuned by the cross-side RTU or by the:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the cross-side RTU or the FMS may also tune the VHF transceiver.

questions.question: The HF communication system consists of how many independent transceivers?
questions.explanation: The HF communication system consists of two independent transceivers: HF 1 and HF 2.

questions.question: HF communication range is described as:

options.A: shorter than VHF but more reliable
options.B: potentially global but less reliable than VHF/UHF

questions.explanation: The PTG states HF range is potentially global but less reliable than conventional VHF and UHF.

questions.question: The HF transceivers provide AM and SSB communications in the frequency range of:
questions.explanation: The HF system operates from 2 to 30 MHz.

questions.question: HF frequencies are selectable in increments of:
questions.explanation: The PTG states HF frequencies are selectable in 100 Hz increments.

questions.question: The two HF transceivers may be operated simultaneously in:
questions.explanation: Both HF transceivers may operate simultaneously in receive mode, but only one may transmit at a time.

questions.question: Only one HF transceiver can transmit at a time because:

options.A: there is only one audio control panel
options.B: antenna couplers exchange interlock data so only one transceiver is connected to the antenna for transmit
options.D: the IAPS inhibits one HF whenever the other is powered

questions.explanation: The antenna couplers exchange interlock data to ensure only one transceiver is connected to the antenna for transmit.

questions.question: The HF antenna is an integral part of the leading edge of the:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the HF antenna is integral to the leading edge of the vertical stabilizer.

questions.question: The antenna coupler matches the impedance of the transceiver to the antenna as different frequencies are:
questions.explanation: The antenna coupler matches impedance as frequencies are selected and keyed.

questions.question: During HF transmissions, high voltages and currents are present in the area of the:
questions.explanation: The PTG cautions that high voltages and currents are present in the HF antenna area during transmissions.

questions.question: SELCAL allows the crew to avoid continuously monitoring:
questions.explanation: SELCAL monitors selected HF frequencies so the crew does not need to continuously monitor HF audio.

questions.question: If the aircraft’s SELCAL code is detected, the crew receives:

options.B: an EICAS message and an audio alert

questions.explanation: The PTG states an EICAS message and audio alert are triggered.

questions.question: A SELCAL HF 1/2 message is canceled when:

options.B: the appropriate HF transceiver is keyed to transmit
options.D: the audio panel is set to INT/SVC

questions.explanation: The SELCAL message resets when the appropriate HF transceiver is keyed to reply.

questions.question: The radio tuning system supports full cross-side and reversionary tuning, allowing:

options.A: only on-side control of on-side radios
options.B: single-point control of both on-side and cross-side radios

questions.explanation: The PTG states it allows single-point control of both on-side and cross-side radios from either pilot position.

questions.question: The radio tuning system consists of two RTUs and one:
questions.explanation: The radio tuning system consists of two RTUs and one reversionary/inhibit panel.

questions.question: Each RTU has how many line select keys?
questions.explanation: Each RTU has eight line select keys, four on each side of the display.

questions.question: The RTU has dedicated pushbuttons labeled IDENT, DME-H, and:
questions.explanation: The three dedicated RTU pushbuttons are IDENT, DME-H, and 1/2.

questions.question: The tuning knob cluster on the RTU uses two concentric knobs that are:
questions.explanation: The PTG states both concentric tuning knobs are rate-aided.

questions.question: The tuning window on the RTU is displayed as:

options.A: a circle around the active frequency
options.B: a rectangle around the frequency/code to be tuned
options.C: a flashing cursor on the title line
options.D: an amber box around the page title

questions.explanation: The tuning window is displayed as a rectangle around the frequency or code to be tuned.

questions.question: If there is no RTU key or knob activity for 20 seconds, the display page:

options.B: reverts to the primary top level display

questions.explanation: After 20 seconds of inactivity, the RTU reverts to the primary top level display.

questions.question: The RTU display is two-level tiered for:

options.B: VHF COM/NAV, ADF, ATC, and TCAS

questions.explanation: The PTG states VHF COM/NAV, ADF, ATC, and TCAS are two-level tiered.

questions.question: The RTU display is three-level tiered for:
questions.explanation: COM 3 uses a three-level hierarchy.

questions.question: The RTU display is four-level tiered for:
questions.explanation: HF COM uses a four-level hierarchy including preset pages.

questions.question: The RTU 1/2 pushbutton is used to:

options.B: select left side (1) or right side (2) radios for tuning
options.C: select HF 1 or HF 2 power levels
options.D: switch between TCAS and ATC modes

questions.explanation: The 1/2 pushbutton selects which side radios are being tuned.

questions.question: The DME-H pushbutton is used to:

options.A: hold the current DME frequency and allow the nav receiver to be retuned independently
options.D: switch DME between AUTO and OFF

questions.explanation: DME-H holds the DME at its current frequency while the NAV receiver can be independently retuned.

questions.question: The IDENT pushbutton is used at ATC controller request to:

options.B: cause an additional identification pattern on ATC ground radar

questions.explanation: IDENT causes an additional identification pattern to appear on the ATC radar screen.

questions.question: On the reversionary/inhibit panel, pressing RTU 1 INHIBIT will:

options.A: disable RTU 1 and enable direct cross-side tuning of off-side radios by RTU 2

questions.explanation: RTU 1 INHIBIT disables RTU 1 and lets RTU 2 directly control the off-side radios.

questions.question: Pressing FMS TUNE INHIB on the reversionary/inhibit panel will:

options.B: prevent RTUs from being tuned by either FMS

questions.explanation: FMS TUNE INHIB prevents RTUs from being tuned by either FMS.

questions.question: If the RTU fails self-test during power-up, the screen will:

options.A: show FAIL in amber but continue operating
options.B: go blank and display RTU FAILURE in the center
options.D: revert automatically to the other RTU

questions.explanation: A failed self-test blanks the screen and displays RTU FAILURE.

questions.question: If RTU internal temperature exceeds its threshold, the annunciation shown is:
questions.explanation: The RTU TEMP annunciation is displayed for overtemperature.

questions.question: If echoed data does not match the newly selected value within two seconds, the RTU display replaces it with:
questions.explanation: The PTG states the data is replaced by dashes if the echoed value does not match within two seconds.

questions.question: The Cockpit Voice Recorder records flight crew conversations, radio communications, and flight compartment sounds on three separate channels plus a fourth channel used for:
questions.explanation: The fourth CVR channel is used for synchronization with the flight data recorder.

questions.question: The CVR solid-state memory stores audio data for a minimum of:
questions.explanation: The CVR stores a minimum of 30 minutes of audio.

questions.question: The CVR ERASE pushbutton will bulk-erase the memory provided:

options.A: the aircraft is airborne and both generators are on
options.B: the parking brake is set and WOW interlocks are closed

questions.explanation: Erase is permitted only with parking brake set and WOW interlocks closed.

questions.question: The Flight Data Recorder is automatically turned on when:

options.B: the right engine start switch is pushed

questions.explanation: The PTG states the FDR is automatically turned on when the right engine start switch is pushed.

questions.question: The Challenger 604 AFCS is a subsystem of the:
questions.explanation: The Automatic Flight Control System is a subsystem of the Collins Pro Line 4 avionics system.

questions.question: The AFCS includes dual two-axis autopilots, integrated dual flight directors, dual yaw dampers, and:
questions.explanation: The AFCS includes automatic pitch trim control in addition to dual autopilots, dual flight directors, and dual yaw dampers.

questions.question: The AFCS consists primarily of four FCCs, one FCP, aileron and elevator servos, and:

options.B: two linear actuators for yaw damping

questions.explanation: The system includes two linear actuators for the rudder yaw damping function.

questions.question: The four Flight Control Computers are contained in the:
questions.explanation: The Integrated Avionics Processor System contains the four FCCs.

questions.question: The FCCs process information from the IRS, ADCs, navigation systems, and:
questions.explanation: The FCCs process information from the IRS, ADCs, NAV systems, and cockpit control panels.

questions.question: The FCCs provide output to the PFD flight directors, autopilot servomotors, yaw damper actuators, and:
questions.explanation: The FCCs provide output to the Horizontal Stabilizer Trim Control Unit.

questions.question: The AFCS SEL switch selects the active FCC pair for steering commands to the servos and HSTCU. AFCS 1 uses:
questions.explanation: AFCS 1 uses FCC 1A and 2A.

questions.question: AFCS 2 uses:
questions.explanation: AFCS 2 uses FCC 1B and 2B.

questions.question: How many AFCS FCC systems can be active at one time?
questions.explanation: Only one FCC pair system, AFCS 1 or AFCS 2, can be active at any time.

questions.question: Each FCC is capable of generating independent:
questions.explanation: Each FCC can generate independent flight director commands.

questions.question: The Challenger 604 autopilot is a:
questions.explanation: The autopilot is a dual-channel, two-axis, fail-passive system.

questions.question: The autopilot controls the aircraft’s:
questions.explanation: The autopilot controls pitch and roll. Yaw damping is provided by the yaw damper system.

questions.question: The autopilot is engaged by pressing the:
questions.explanation: The autopilot is engaged by pressing the AP ENG button on the Flight Control Panel.

questions.question: Autopilot engagement is indicated by green indicator lights beside the AP button and:

options.A: a green AP annunciation on each PFD
options.B: an amber AP light on EICAS
options.D: a red AP box on the PFD

questions.explanation: Autopilot engagement is indicated by the AP annunciation on both PFDs and green lights beside the AP ENG button.

questions.question: If no FD modes are selected before autopilot engagement, the autopilot follows basic lateral and vertical modes of:
questions.explanation: If no FD modes are commanded, autopilot defaults to ROLL and PTCH.

questions.question: One requirement for autopilot engagement is that at least:
questions.explanation: At least one yaw damper must be engaged for autopilot engagement.

questions.question: Another autopilot engagement requirement is that the AP DISC switch-bar must be:
questions.explanation: The AP DISC switch-bar must be in the normal position.

questions.question: The autopilot cannot be engaged if:
questions.explanation: Adverse rates, excessive G-load, or excessive attitudes prevent autopilot engagement.

questions.question: The aileron servo is located on the control cable run for the:
questions.explanation: The aileron servo is installed in the right aileron control cable run.

questions.question: The elevator servo is located in the:
questions.explanation: The elevator servo is located in the left elevator control cable run.

questions.question: Automatic pitch trim is enabled whenever:
questions.explanation: Automatic pitch trim is enabled whenever the autopilot is engaged.

questions.question: Automatic pitch trim commands are sent to the:
questions.explanation: Autopilot pitch trim commands are issued to the HSTCU.

questions.question: Protracted autopilot trim operation causes the:
questions.explanation: Protracted trim operation causes the trim clacker to sound.

questions.question: Autopilot pitch trim failure is annunciated by which EICAS caution?
questions.explanation: Autopilot pitch trim failure produces the AP PITCH TRIM caution.

questions.question: A significant elevator mistrim is shown on the PFD by an amber:
questions.explanation: Elevator mistrim is shown by an amber E on the upper left of the PFD.

questions.question: A significant aileron mistrim is shown on the PFD by an amber:
questions.explanation: Aileron mistrim is shown by an amber A on the PFD.

questions.question: A significant nose-up autopilot mistrim condition produces which EICAS caution?
questions.explanation: AP TRIM IS NU indicates the autopilot is counteracting a significant nose-up mistrim.

questions.question: A left wing down autopilot mistrim condition produces which EICAS caution?
questions.explanation: AP TRIM IS LWD indicates a left wing down mistrim condition.

questions.question: Turbulence mode is available only when:
questions.explanation: TURB mode is available only when the autopilot is engaged.

questions.question: When TURB mode is active, the FCC:
questions.explanation: TURB mode reduces autopilot gains for turbulent flight conditions.

questions.question: TURB mode is cleared by selecting the button again, on-side LOC capture, or:
questions.explanation: TURB mode is cleared by pressing TURB again, on-side LOC capture, or autopilot disengagement.

questions.question: The normal method of manually disengaging the autopilot is by pressing the red:
questions.explanation: The autopilot is normally manually disengaged with the red AP/SP DISC button on either control wheel.

questions.question: The autopilot may also be manually disengaged by all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Selecting NAV does not by itself disengage the autopilot.

questions.question: A manual autopilot disengagement causes the PFD AP annunciation to:

options.B: turn red and flash for 5 seconds

questions.explanation: Following manual disengagement, the AP annunciation turns red and flashes for 5 seconds.

questions.question: The aural warning for autopilot disengagement is the:
questions.explanation: Autopilot disengagement is accompanied by the cavalry charge aural warning.

questions.question: A monitored autopilot disengagement may occur due to loss of FCC power, dual yaw damper failure, excessive attitude, stick shaker activation, or:

options.D: failure of one weather radar channel

questions.explanation: Loss of either IRS system input is one of the monitored autopilot disengagement conditions.

questions.question: A monitored autopilot disengagement differs from a manual one because the flashing AP warning and aural:

options.B: continue until canceled by the crew

questions.explanation: In a monitored disengagement, the warnings continue until acknowledged.

questions.question: Each yaw damper is a dual-channel, fully independent system that provides yaw damping, turn coordination, and:
questions.explanation: Each yaw damper provides yaw damping, turn entry, steady-state turn coordination, and turn exit.

questions.question: The yaw dampers operate:
questions.explanation: The yaw dampers operate independently of the autopilot.

questions.question: Normally, for all phases of flight, the crew should engage:
questions.explanation: Normally both yaw dampers are engaged for all phases of flight.

questions.question: If both yaw damper channels are disengaged, the PFD displays an amber:
questions.explanation: Both yaw dampers disengaged produces an amber YD annunciation on the PFD.

questions.question: If both yaw damper channels are disengaged, which EICAS caution appears?
questions.explanation: A YAW DAMPER caution appears when both channels are disengaged.

questions.question: A single inoperative yaw damper channel is indicated by which EICAS status message?
questions.explanation: A single failed or disengaged yaw damper channel produces YD 1 INOP or YD 2 INOP.

questions.question: The flight directors provide pitch and roll guidance by means of:
questions.explanation: FD guidance is shown by magenta inverted V-shaped command bars on the ADI.

questions.question: At power-up, both flight directors are:
questions.explanation: At power-up, both flight directors are off.

questions.question: After flight director mode selection with the autopilot disengaged, the default active flight director is:

options.C: the side with the lower altitude selector

questions.explanation: FD 1 is the default active flight director.

questions.question: Pressing the XFR button on the FCP makes active:
questions.explanation: The XFR button transfers control so FD 2 becomes the active flight director.

questions.question: Dual independent flight director operation occurs in takeoff, go-around, and:
questions.explanation: Dual independent FD operation occurs in takeoff, go-around, and approach modes.

questions.question: The FMA displays active/captured modes in:
questions.explanation: Active and captured modes are shown in green.

questions.question: The FMA displays armed modes in:
questions.explanation: Armed lateral and vertical modes are shown in white.

questions.question: ROLL mode is the basic lateral mode and will command wings level if bank angle at selection is less than:
questions.explanation: If bank is less than 5°, ROLL mode commands wings level and heading hold.

questions.question: In ROLL mode, using FD SYNC allows the pilot to set a roll reference up to:
questions.explanation: ROLL mode allows a synchronized bank reference up to 30°.

questions.question: HDG mode turns the aircraft toward the heading bug using:
questions.explanation: On selection, heading mode turns the aircraft via the shortest turn to the heading bug.

questions.question: Half Bank mode reduces the roll limit to half the normal value and is automatically selected above:
questions.explanation: Automatic half bank is selected at and above 31,600 feet MSL.

questions.question: The NAV button can arm and capture VOR, LOC, or:
questions.explanation: NAV mode can be used for VOR, LOC, and FMS navigation.

questions.question: In VOR station passage while tracking, the system provides:
questions.explanation: During VOR station passage, the system uses memorized heading and displays DR for dead reckoning.

questions.question: Approach mode can be used for ILS approaches and for FMS-based:
questions.explanation: Approach mode is used for localizer-based ILS or FMS-based nonprecision approaches.

questions.question: Back Course mode tracks the localizer using:
questions.explanation: Back Course mode uses reverse-sensing localizer tracking.

questions.question: Pressing TOGA on the ground selects lateral and vertical:
questions.explanation: On the ground, TOGA selects takeoff modes.

questions.question: Pressing TOGA in flight selects lateral and vertical:
questions.explanation: In flight, TOGA selects go-around modes and disengages the autopilot.

questions.question: The aural and visual warning system provides the flight crew with aural, visual, and tactile indications of:

options.B: potentially unsafe conditions and system malfunctions

questions.explanation: The system provides indications of potentially unsafe conditions, airplane configurations, engine data, system malfunctions, and non-normal situations.

questions.question: The function of EICAS is to display engine instruments and provide visual and aural:
questions.explanation: EICAS displays engine instruments and provides visual and aural crew-alert messages plus real-time interpretation of aircraft system operation.

questions.question: How many EICAS displays are installed?
questions.explanation: Two EICAS displays present the data on different selectable display pages.

questions.question: The EICAS control panel is located on the:
questions.explanation: The EICAS control panel (ECP) is located on the center pedestal.

questions.question: Engine indications are provided on the EICAS:
questions.explanation: Engine indications are displayed on the EICAS primary page.

questions.question: The Crew Alerting System (CAS) prioritizes messages by:

options.B: order of occurrence and order of importance

questions.explanation: CAS prioritizes messages by order of occurrence and order of importance.

questions.question: The EICAS control panel remains illuminated during a complete:
questions.explanation: The panel remains illuminated during a complete AC power failure.

questions.question: During a complete AC power failure, which ECP keys remain operational?
questions.explanation: PRI, STAT, STEP, and CAS remain operational during a complete AC power failure.

questions.question: EICAS display No. 1 (ED 1) is installed:

options.A: on the right of the center instrument panel
options.B: on the left of the center instrument panel

questions.explanation: ED 1 is installed on the left of the center instrument panel.

questions.question: EICAS display No. 2 (ED 2) is installed:

options.A: on the right of the center instrument panel
options.B: on the left of the center instrument panel

questions.explanation: ED 2 is installed on the right of the center panel.

questions.question: ED 1 displays which page by default?
questions.explanation: ED 1 displays the primary page by default.

questions.question: ED 2 is defaulted to the:
questions.explanation: ED 2 is defaulted to the status page.

questions.question: The data concentrator unit (DCU) is described as the:
questions.explanation: The PTG describes the DCU as the heart of the EICAS.

questions.question: In a standard configuration, how many DCUs are installed?
questions.explanation: In a standard configuration, there are two DCUs installed.

questions.question: An optional third DCU may be installed and is designated:
questions.explanation: The optional third DCU is labeled DCU 3.

questions.question: If a partial or complete failure of a DCU occurs, displayed data is protected by:
questions.explanation: System redundancy ensures that no displayed data is lost.

questions.question: The DCUs receive inputs from all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: The listed DCU inputs include engines, landing gear, flaps, spoilers, APU, flight controls, avionics, SPS, GPWS, TCAS, CAS, and ADC altitude alerting. Weather radar antenna tilt is not listed.

questions.question: The DCUs transmit processed data on the ARINC bus to the EICAS displays, LDU, AECU, FDR, and:
questions.explanation: The DCUs transmit data to the EICAS displays, Lamp Driver Unit, Audio Electronic Control Unit, Flight Data Recorder, and Maintenance Diagnostic Computer.

questions.question: The Lamp Driver Unit (LDU) receives information from the DCUs and controls:
questions.explanation: The LDU controls associated panel and glareshield switch/light illumination.

questions.question: Testing the LDU and panel lamps requires:
questions.explanation: AC power is required to perform the lamp test.

questions.question: When the DCUs generate a warning message, the master warning switch/lights flash:
questions.explanation: Warning messages cause the two master warning switch/lights to flash red.

questions.question: A master warning is always accompanied by a:
questions.explanation: A triple attenson always accompanies the master warning lights.

questions.question: Pressing either master warning switch/light will:

options.B: reset the flashing red light and the audio alerts

questions.explanation: Pressing the switch/light resets the flashing red light and the audio alerts.

questions.question: When the DCUs generate a caution message, the master caution switch/lights flash:
questions.explanation: Caution messages cause the master caution switch/lights to flash amber.

questions.question: A master caution is always accompanied by a:
questions.explanation: A single attenson always accompanies the master caution lights.

questions.question: The AURAL WARN TEST switch is located on the:
questions.explanation: The AURAL WARN TEST switch is located on the miscellaneous test panel.

questions.question: The AURAL WARN TEST switch is:
questions.explanation: It is a two-position switch spring-loaded to neutral.

questions.question: Selecting the AURAL WARN TEST switch to position 1 or 2 and releasing it will sequentially sound warnings from:
questions.explanation: Position 1 or 2 tests DCU 1 or DCU 2 aural and voice warnings sequentially.

questions.question: Which sound corresponds to STALL?
questions.explanation: The warbler is the stall warning sound.

questions.question: Which sound corresponds to FIRE WARNING?
questions.explanation: The fire bell indicates fire warning.

questions.question: Which sound corresponds to VMO/MMO and flap overspeed?
questions.explanation: The clacker is used for VMO/MMO and flap overspeed.

questions.question: The "drum roll" clacker corresponds to:
questions.explanation: The clacker (drum roll) is used for excessive stabilizer trim movement.

questions.question: Which aural warning corresponds to autopilot disconnect?
questions.explanation: The cavalry charge indicates autopilot disconnect.

questions.question: Which aural warning corresponds to gear not down?
questions.explanation: The horn corresponds to gear not down.

questions.question: The c-chord sound corresponds to:
questions.explanation: The c-chord is the altitude alert.

questions.question: The double c-chord sound corresponds to:
questions.explanation: The double c-chord is used for vertical track alert.

questions.question: The AUDIO WARNING panel is located on the:
questions.explanation: The AUDIO WARNING panel is on the copilot side console.

questions.question: The DISABLE switch/lights on the AUDIO WARNING panel are used to:

options.B: disable and silence the aural warnings of a malfunctioning DCU
options.C: disable warning messages on the primary page

questions.explanation: These switch/lights disable and silence the aural warnings of a malfunctioning DCU.

questions.question: If DCU 1 is selected to DISABLE, aural warning function is provided by:
questions.explanation: When DCU 1 is disabled, DCU 2 provides the aural warning function.

questions.question: GPWS and TCAS aural warnings are:

options.A: disabled by the audio warning panel
options.B: not disabled by the audio warning panel
options.C: disabled only if both DCUs are disabled

questions.explanation: GPWS and TCAS aural warnings are not disabled by selections at the audio warning panel.

questions.question: Selecting PRI on the EICAS control panel causes the primary page to be displayed on:
questions.explanation: Selecting PRI causes the primary page to be displayed on ED 1.

questions.question: Pressing STAT causes the status page to be displayed on:
questions.explanation: Pressing STAT causes the status page to be displayed on ED 2.

questions.question: If the status page is already displayed, pressing STAT once will:

options.B: remove status messages and display a white MSGS icon

questions.explanation: If the status page is already displayed, STAT removes the status messages and displays a white MSGS icon.

questions.question: The STEP button will sequentially step through:

options.C: all available pages including primary, secondary, and synoptic

questions.explanation: STEP sequences through all available pages, including primary, secondary, and synoptic.

questions.question: How many synoptic pages are available?
questions.explanation: There are four synoptic pages.

questions.question: Selecting HYD on the ECP displays the:
questions.explanation: Selecting HYD displays the hydraulic system synoptic.

questions.question: Selecting ELEC once displays the:
questions.explanation: Selecting ELEC displays the AC electrical system first.

questions.question: Selecting ELEC a second time displays the:
questions.explanation: A second selection of ELEC displays the DC electrical system.

questions.question: During flight or on the ground with both engines stabilized at idle, pressing CAS can:

options.A: remove displayed caution messages and show a white MSGS icon

questions.explanation: The CAS button can remove caution messages from view and display a white MSGS icon.

questions.question: Engine indications on the primary page include N1, ITT, N2, fuel flow, oil pressure, oil temperature, and:
questions.explanation: Displayed engine parameters also include fan vibration.

questions.question: Fuel quantity on the primary page includes main tank quantities, auxiliary tank total, tail tank total, and:
questions.explanation: The primary page includes the aircraft’s total fuel quantity readout.

questions.question: Warning and caution messages are presented only on the EICAS:
questions.explanation: Warning and caution messages are presented only on the EICAS primary page.

questions.question: Landing gear and flap information is removed from view after 30 seconds in flight when:

options.B: gear is up and locked, and flaps are up
options.C: gear is down and flaps are up
options.D: gear is in transit and flaps are up

questions.explanation: The landing gear and flap indications disappear after 30 seconds if the gear is up and locked and the flaps are up.

questions.question: Trim settings continuously displayed on the primary page are aileron, horizontal stabilizer, and:
questions.explanation: Aileron, horizontal stabilizer, and rudder trim are continuously displayed.

questions.question: Advisory and status messages are presented only on the EICAS:
questions.explanation: Advisory and status messages are shown only on the EICAS status page.

questions.question: The APU RPM and EGT gauges are displayed on the status page when the:

options.B: APU PWR FUEL switch is selected

questions.explanation: APU RPM and EGT are shown when the APU PWR FUEL switch is selected.

questions.question: Oxygen pressure on the status page is displayed in green when normal, and in amber when below:
questions.explanation: Amber identifies oxygen pressure below 800 psi.

questions.question: On synoptic pages, a normal range indication is shown in:
questions.explanation: On synoptic pages, normal range is shown in green.

questions.question: The most urgent CAS messages are called warnings and are shown in:
questions.explanation: Warnings are the most urgent and are shown in red.

questions.question: A warning message is always accompanied by a triple attenson, flashing master warning lights, and:

options.B: a red light on the faulted control switch or a red indication on EFIS/EICAS/synoptic

questions.explanation: Warning messages are accompanied by triple attenson, flashing master warning lights, and a red light or red indication on the affected display/control.

questions.question: The lighting system provides interior and exterior illumination of the aircraft and also:

options.B: provides information and guidance to passengers in normal and emergency situations

questions.explanation: The lighting system provides interior and exterior illumination and gives passengers information and guidance in normal and emergency situations.

questions.question: The lighting system includes exterior lighting, flight compartment lighting, passenger compartment lighting, service lighting, and:
questions.explanation: The chapter introduction lists emergency lighting as one of the main lighting system groups.

questions.question: The exterior lighting system is controlled from the EXTERNAL LTS panel or the:
questions.explanation: Exterior lighting is controlled from the EXTERNAL LTS panel or the LANDING LTS panel.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as part of the exterior lighting system?
questions.explanation: Emergency exit floor track lights are part of the emergency lighting system, not the exterior lighting system.

questions.question: The navigation lights are located in each wing tip, fuselage tail cone tip, and:

options.B: at the tip of the stabilizer fairing

questions.explanation: Navigation lights are located at each wing tip, the fuselage tail cone tip, and the tip of the stabilizer fairing.

questions.question: A wing tip navigation light assembly contains:

options.B: two navigation lights that work simultaneously
options.C: one navigation light and one landing light

questions.explanation: Each wing tip assembly contains two navigation lights that work simultaneously.

questions.question: The NAV switch controls the:
questions.explanation: The NAV switch controls the wing and tail navigation lights.

questions.question: The anti-collision lights consist of two wing tip strobe lights and:

options.A: one strobe at the tip of the fuselage tail cone
options.C: one strobe on each engine pylon
options.D: two strobes in the wheel wells

questions.explanation: The anti-collision lights include two wing tip strobes and one strobe at the tip of the fuselage tail cone.

questions.question: The A/COLL switch controls the:
questions.explanation: The A/COLL switch controls the anti-collision strobe lights.

questions.question: There are how many wing inspection lights?
questions.explanation: There are two wing inspection lights, one located forward of the root of each wing.

questions.question: The wing inspection lights are located on the fuselage forward of:
questions.explanation: The wing inspection lights are located on the fuselage forward of the root of each wing.

questions.question: The WING switch controls the:

options.B: left and right wing inspection lights

questions.explanation: The WING switch controls both wing inspection lights.

questions.question: There are two red beacon lights located:

options.B: on the top and bottom of the fuselage near the middle of the aircraft
options.D: at the tail cone tip only

questions.explanation: The two red beacon lights are located on the top and bottom of the fuselage near the middle of the aircraft.

questions.question: The BEACON switch controls the:
questions.explanation: The BEACON switch controls the beacon lights.

questions.question: The logo lights are located on top of the aircraft’s:
questions.explanation: The logo lights are located on top of the engine pylons.

questions.question: When selected, the logo lights illuminate the aircraft’s:
questions.explanation: The logo lights illuminate the vertical stabilizer.

questions.question: The LOGO switch controls the:
questions.explanation: The LOGO switch controls the logo lights.

questions.question: How many landing lights are installed on the aircraft?
questions.explanation: There are four landing lights: two in the nose and one at each wing root.

questions.question: The NOSE switch on the LANDING LTS panel controls:
questions.explanation: The NOSE switch controls both nose landing lights simultaneously.

questions.question: The LEFT and RIGHT switches on the LANDING LTS panel control:
questions.explanation: The LEFT and RIGHT switches control the left and right wing landing lights respectively.

questions.question: If the optional pulse lighting system is installed, the pulse selection is made from a modified:

options.B: three-position left and right landing light switch

questions.explanation: With the pulse lighting option, a modified three-position left and right landing light switch is used.

questions.question: Turning on the left or right landing light also turns on the respective:
questions.explanation: Turning on a left or right landing light also turns on the corresponding recognition/taxi light.

questions.question: There are how many recognition/taxi lights?
questions.explanation: There are two recognition/taxi lights, one co-located outboard of each wing landing light.

questions.question: The recognition/taxi lights are located:

options.B: outboard of the wing landing lights

questions.explanation: The recognition/taxi lights are co-located outboard of the wing landing lights.

questions.question: The RECOG/TAXI switch controls:
questions.explanation: The RECOG/TAXI switch controls the recognition/taxi lights.

questions.question: Flight compartment lighting controls are located on how many panels?
questions.explanation: Flight compartment lighting controls are located on four panels.

questions.question: The pilot’s and copilot’s lighting panels are located on the:
questions.explanation: The pilot’s and copilot’s lighting panels are located on the lower center pedestal.

questions.question: The pilot’s and copilot’s lighting panels are:
questions.explanation: The pilot’s and copilot’s lighting panels are identical.

questions.question: On the pilot’s and copilot’s lighting panels, the DSPL control knob controls the intensity of the:
questions.explanation: The DSPL knob controls the intensity of the PFD and MFD on the respective side.

questions.question: Each PFD or MFD may also be adjusted individually with a recessed knob located:

options.B: below the bottom right-hand corner of each screen

questions.explanation: Each display has an individual recessed brightness knob below the bottom right-hand corner.

questions.question: The INTEG control knobs on the pilot’s/copilot’s panels control the intensity of the:

options.B: integral lighting for the instrument panel and side panel

questions.explanation: The INTEG knobs control instrument panel and side panel integral lighting.

questions.question: The FLOOD control knobs on the pilot’s/copilot’s panels control the intensity of the flood lighting located:

options.B: above the instrument panel and side panel

questions.explanation: The FLOOD control knobs control the flood lighting above the instrument panel and side panel.

questions.question: The FLOOR lighting toggle switches control lights located above the:
questions.explanation: The FLOOR switches control incandescent lights above the rudder pedals.

questions.question: The center pedestal DSPL control knob adjusts the intensity of EICAS 1 and 2, the two FMS displays, and the:
questions.explanation: The center pedestal DSPL knob adjusts EICAS 1 and 2 plus the two FMS and RTU displays.

questions.question: The center pedestal INTEG control knob controls the intensity of the integral lighting for:

options.B: all panels on the center pedestal

questions.explanation: The center pedestal INTEG knob controls integral lighting for all center pedestal panels.

questions.question: The center pedestal FLOOD control knob controls the flood lighting located:
questions.explanation: The center pedestal FLOOD control knob controls flood lighting under the center instrument panel.

questions.question: The CB PNL control knob controls the integral lighting for all flight compartment:
questions.explanation: The CB PNL knob controls circuit breaker panel integral lighting.

questions.question: The OVHD control knob controls the intensity of the integral lighting for the:
questions.explanation: The OVHD knob is the dimmer for overhead panel integral lighting.

questions.question: With the BATT MASTER switch on, which of the following illuminates full bright and cannot be dimmed?
questions.explanation: The APU panel, electric panel, EICAS control panel, FIREX MONITOR/SPS test panel, standby compass, and standby instruments illuminate full bright and cannot be dimmed.

questions.question: The IND LTS toggle switch controls the bright/dim setting of the flight compartment:
questions.explanation: The IND LTS switch controls the bright/dim setting of flight compartment indicator lights.

questions.question: The IND LTS switch has no effect on:
questions.explanation: The IND LTS switch does not affect EFIS or EICAS displays.

questions.question: AC power is required to:
questions.explanation: The PTG specifically notes that AC power is required to dim lights.

questions.question: Chart holder and map lights at the pilot’s and copilot’s positions are controlled by their respective:
questions.explanation: Chart holder and map lights are controlled by a dimmer knob from OFF to BRIGHT.

questions.question: During lamp test, LAMP TEST 1 illuminates:
questions.explanation: LAMP TEST 1 illuminates the FCP left-side lights.

questions.question: During lamp test, LAMP TEST 2 illuminates:
questions.explanation: LAMP TEST 2 illuminates the FCP right-side lights.

questions.question: Which lamp does NOT illuminate during the lamp test sequence?

options.A: ADG AUTO DEPLOY CONTROL TEST lamp

questions.explanation: The ADG AUTO DEPLOY CONTROL TEST lamp does not illuminate during the lamp test sequence.

questions.question: AC power is required to perform a successful test of:
questions.explanation: AC power is required to successfully test both LDU channels.

questions.question: The DOME switch controls the overhead light at the:
questions.explanation: The DOME switch controls the overhead light at the entrance of the passenger door.

questions.question: There are how many ways to control the stair lights?
questions.explanation: The stair lights can be controlled in three ways.

questions.question: The stair lights can be controlled by the BOARD switch, a switch near the top of the passenger door, and:

options.A: a pushbutton on the center pedestal
options.B: a toggle switch or pushbutton on the left-hand side of the stairs

questions.explanation: The third method is a toggle switch or pushbutton on the left-hand side of the stairs.

questions.question: Passenger NO SMKG and SEAT BELTS signs are controlled by switches located on the:
questions.explanation: The NO SMKG and SEAT BELTS switches are on the Passenger Signs/Emergency Lights panel.

questions.question: Each time a passenger sign comes on, an:
questions.explanation: Each time a passenger sign illuminates, an electronic chime is heard.

questions.question: In AUTO, the SEAT BELTS signs illuminate automatically when:

options.B: the flaps are selected greater than 0

questions.explanation: With flaps selected greater than 0, the SEAT BELTS signs illuminate automatically.

questions.question: In AUTO, both NO SMKG and SEAT BLTS signs illuminate when:

options.A: the landing gear is selected down

questions.explanation: When the landing gear is selected down, both passenger signs illuminate in AUTO.

questions.question: In AUTO, both NO SMKG and SEAT BLTS signs illuminate when cabin altitude is greater than:
questions.explanation: With cabin altitude greater than 10,000 feet, both signs illuminate automatically.

questions.question: Lighting is provided underneath each pylon for the cargo bay door area, water-fill service door, and:
questions.explanation: Service lighting is provided for the cargo bay door area, water-fill service door, and toilet service door.

questions.question: The nose landing gear wheel well service light is installed on the:

options.A: aft wall of the wheel well
options.B: forward wall of the wheel well

questions.explanation: The nose landing gear wheel well service light is installed on the forward wall of the wheel well.

questions.question: There are how many service lights installed in the aft equipment compartment?
questions.explanation: There are three service lights installed in the aft equipment compartment.

questions.question: The emergency lights are powered by two self-contained rechargeable battery packs that each provide approximately:
questions.explanation: Each battery pack provides approximately 15 minutes of power.

questions.question: When the EMER LTS switch is selected to ARM, the emergency lights automatically illuminate if electrical power is lost on the:

options.A: AC essential bus or DC essential bus

questions.explanation: In ARM, the emergency lighting system illuminates automatically if power is lost on the AC essential bus or DC essential bus.

questions.question: The Emergency Equipment chapter covers the oxygen system, emergency exits, locating devices, and:
questions.explanation: The chapter covers oxygen system, emergency exits, locating devices, and miscellaneous emergency equipment.

questions.question: This chapter deals only with:
questions.explanation: The PTG states that this chapter deals only with factory-installed equipment.

questions.question: The factory-installed oxygen system is a:
questions.explanation: The Challenger 604 uses an 1850-psi gaseous oxygen system.

questions.question: The oxygen system supplies oxygen to the flight crew and passengers during:
questions.explanation: The oxygen system supplies oxygen to the crew and passengers during an emergency.

questions.question: The oxygen system consists of the flight compartment, passenger compartment, therapeutic system, and:
questions.explanation: The PTG lists flight compartment, passenger compartment, therapeutic, and portable oxygen bottle subsystems.

questions.question: The single-bottle oxygen system consists of one storage bottle, a ground servicing panel, and:
questions.explanation: The single-bottle system includes one storage bottle, a ground servicing panel, and associated masks/regulators.

questions.question: In the single-bottle system, the oxygen storage bottle capacity is:
questions.explanation: The single-bottle system uses one 1260-liter (50.0 cubic feet) oxygen bottle.

questions.question: In the single-bottle system, the oxygen storage bottle is located in the:
questions.explanation: The single oxygen bottle is located in the forward avionics bay.

questions.question: The dual-bottle oxygen system consists of two storage bottles, a ground servicing panel, and:
questions.explanation: The dual-bottle system includes two storage bottles, a ground servicing panel, and associated masks/regulators.

questions.question: In the dual-bottle system, each oxygen bottle has a capacity of:
